ci: Stories are shared in different ways.
lo: Imagining stories together; Ways stories are shared; Connecting with stories

s.d: Students create and perform their own stories by choosing characters, settings, and props within Puppet Pals, bringing familiar or original tales to life through digital puppetry. They record their performances to share with classmates, exploring how stories can be expressed through voice and movement in a fun, interactive way that supports imagination and narrative skills.

Students become Mystery Storytellers by recording their own imaginative audio stories that explore the central idea 'Stories are shared in different ways.' Using Adobe Express Podcasting, they add sound effects and music to bring their narratives to life, developing oral language skills and creative expression in a fun, accessible format. Teachers can share these podcasts with families to celebrate each child's unique voice and storytelling style. https://podcast.adobe.com

Students bring their favourite story characters to life by taking photos or drawings and using ChatterPix Kids to record their voices telling a part of the story. This activity helps Prep learners explore how stories are shared through voice and animation, encouraging creativity and oral storytelling as they imagine and express stories together.

Students document their story-creation process by capturing photos and short videos of their developmental play (using costumes, puppets, Lego, etc.) and uploading them to Seesaw. They can add voice reflections about their stories, allowing families to see and hear the diversity of storytelling in the classroom.

Students illustrate their own imaginative story scenes inspired by the central idea that stories are shared in different ways, using Sketchbook?s simple drawing tools to bring characters and settings to life. As they experiment with colours, shapes, and textures, learners express emotions and narrative moments visually, creating unique storyboards that capture the mood and flow of their favourite tales.

Students work in small groups to design and build a large, tactile 'adventure map' for a familiar or original story using cardboard, fabric scraps, recycled materials, and craft supplies. Each group creates 3D settings (forests, houses, rivers, caves) and props that represent key story locations or events. They then program a Bee-Bot or Sphero Indi to travel across the map, stopping at each location to trigger a storytelling moment?students use puppets, costumes, or their own voices to narrate or act out that part of the story. This project combines hands-on building, collaborative storytelling, and basic robotics, allowing students to express stories physically and digitally while practicing sequencing and creative problem-solving through the Design Cycle.

plannerText: Prep students explore the central idea 'Stories are shared in different ways' through imaginative play, daily storytelling, retelling familiar stories, and hands-on activities using props, costumes, puppets, and digital tools. The unit emphasizes student agency, creativity, and multimodal expression, including a Mystery Storyteller program, narrative writing, and an Indigenous storytelling excursion.

plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Stories are shared in different ways.

LINES OF INQUIRY: Imagining stories together; Ways stories are shared; Connecting with stories

KEY CONCEPTS: Responsibility, form

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Showing a mystery box (with costumes inside) as provocation; exploring imaginative story creation during developmental play sessions using finger puppets, dress ups, and sequencing story games; during literacy lessons each week, retelling stories and reading different story books; starting "mystery storytellers" in Week 2.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: Exploring imaginative story creation with finger puppets, dress ups, and sequencing story games; retelling stories during literacy lessons; reading different story books; "mystery storytellers" activity in Week 2; PSPE activities where athletics events are introduced through imaginative contexts, encouraging students to connect movement to ideas and feelings; group activities like relays and team games to show that stories can be shared through actions, teamwork, and play.

ASSESSMENT: Not specified.

TEACHER NOTES: Not specified.

ci: Everyone has a role to play in the community.
lo: My communities; Contributing to the local community; Connections in my community

s.d: Learners create digital puppet shows in Puppet Pals, role-playing different community helpers and school staff to demonstrate their understanding of roles and responsibilities. Students record their voices and animate characters, sharing their collaborative stories with the class to celebrate the diversity of their school community.

Students take photos of staff members or community helpers (with permission) or draw their own, then use ChatterPix Kids to animate the images and record short voice messages about each person's role in the community, fostering understanding of diverse contributions.

Students create a digital portfolio on Seesaw where each child uploads a drawing or photo of a community helper they have learned about, records a short voice message explaining the helper?s role, and shares why that role is important to their community. Teachers guide students to collaborate by commenting and encouraging each other?s contributions, building a vibrant class collection that celebrates everyone?s role in the community.

Students create simple animated stories in ScratchJR where characters take on different community roles, demonstrating how everyone contributes to the school community. Learners program their characters to move between places like the library, classroom, and office, narrating their role and why it is important, reinforcing the central idea through interactive storytelling.

Guide students to create vibrant visual collages in PicCollage representing the different roles and connections in their school community, using photos, stickers, and drawings. These can be printed or shared digitally to celebrate community diversity.

Students work in small groups to select a school community role (e.g., librarian, gardener, crossing guard). Using recycled materials, craft supplies, and simple mechanisms (wheels, levers), each group builds a physical model of their chosen helper and their workspace. They then integrate a Sphero Indi robot as the 'helper'?decorating it to match their creation?and design a hands-on challenge course using cardboard and props that simulates a real task (e.g., delivering books, watering plants, helping students cross a 'street'). Students use color tiles to code the Indi to complete the helper's job, iterating on both the physical course and their coding as they test and refine their designs. The process follows the Design Cycle: Imagine (choose a role and brainstorm), Plan (sketch and gather materials), Create (build and code), and Improve (test and tweak).

plannerText: Prep students explore the central idea 'Everyone has a role to play in the community' by investigating roles within their school, creating wonderings, interviewing staff, and sharing their learning through videos and presentations. Activities include guided walks, role-play, collaborative projects, and reflecting on how individuals contribute to the community.

plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Everyone has a role to play in the community.

LINES OF INQUIRY: My communities; Contributing to the local community; Connections in my community

KEY CONCEPTS: Form, connection

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Week 1 ? Photos of staff in the school: what is the role of these people? Students create drawings of each person. Week 2 ? Photos of staff in the school: creating wonderings about these people.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Read picture story books about schools (e.g., Whose School Is This?)
- Class discussion: Who helps make our school a great place?
- Create a mind map of people we know in our school community
- Walk around the school to observe different areas and people
- Read stories about teachers and their roles; shared oral discussion: "What does a teacher do all day?"
- Draw and label what we think a teacher's job involves
- Wonder Wall: Add questions about teachers and other staff
- Guided school tour; discuss the purpose of different areas (library, office, canteen, etc.)
- Add wonderings to the Wonder Wall: "Who works here? What do they do?"
- Choose key staff (principal, librarian, office staff, cleaner, groundskeeper); brainstorm and record questions to ask them
- Role-play: "What if I were the principal?"
- In small groups, visit and interview different staff members; practice taking turns asking questions and listening; reflect and share what was learned and what surprised them
- Present learning to the school at assembly

ASSESSMENT: 
- Students created their own video to share with the school community after creating their own wonderings
- Assembly presentation to communicate understanding
- Evidence includes participation in interviews, video creation, and oral presentations

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Strong emphasis on inquiry-based, experiential, and reflective strategies
- Wonder wall activities support student-driven questioning and responsive learning
- Collaboration with specialists (Music: matching sounds to staff roles; PE: group cooperation and teamwork; Digital: video recording with Mr Holt)
- Opportunities for agency: students choose how to represent learning (video, drawing, oral)
- Transdisciplinary connections: literacy (vocabulary, oral language), digital technology/media (video production), social capability (empathy, appreciation for others' roles)
- Action opportunities: fostering a sense of belonging, responsibility, and agency within the school community
- Teacher reflection notes highlight the value of authentic experiences, real audiences, and scaffolding for student confidence and participation

ci: Scientists follow a method to discover new things.
lo: The scientific process; Observing my world; Drawing conclusions

s.d: Students use Sky Map on iPads to explore the day and night sky, identifying the sun, moon, and stars as part of their inquiry into scientific observation. They track changes over time and connect their real-world sky observations to digital constellations, sparking curiosity about patterns and cycles in nature.

Empower students to document their Nature Detective Walk and Weather Watchers activities by capturing photos and short videos with the iPad Camera, then uploading them to Seesaw. Students can annotate their observations with voice recordings or drawings, making their scientific thinking visible and accessible to families.

After the Ice Melting Challenge or Rolling Ramp Investigation, students use ChatterPix Kids to animate photos of their experiment materials or 'scientist characters,' recording their own voices to explain predictions, results, and conclusions in a playful, memorable way.

Students create digital collages that document their weekly experiments?such as nature walks, ice melting, and ramp tests?by combining photos, drawings, and simple labels to show their observations and conclusions. This hands-on activity helps Prep learners visually organise their scientific process in a fun, age-appropriate way that encourages reflection and sharing.

Have students use ScratchJr to visually sequence the steps of a science experiment (e.g., the process of melting ice or rolling objects down ramps). They can animate simple characters to model 'predict, test, observe, record,' reinforcing the scientific process through coding and storytelling.

Students work in teams to design and build imaginative 'weather machines' that demonstrate a chosen weather phenomenon (rain, wind, or snow) using recycled materials, cardboard tubes, fabric, and craft supplies. For example, a rain machine might use a hand-cranked mechanism to drop 'raindrops' (beads or pom-poms), while a wind machine could use a fan made from paper and a small motor (with adult help). Once built, students use Bee-Bots to 'deliver' weather reports by programming them to travel between different weather stations on a large, collaboratively painted map. Students test, refine, and present how their machines mimic real weather, documenting their process with photos and simple voice recordings on iPads.

plannerText: Prep students explore the scientific method through hands-on experiences such as nature detective walks, weather observations, ice melting challenges, ramp investigations, and mystery material explorations. The unit emphasizes observing, recording, predicting, testing, and drawing conclusions, fostering inquiry and scientific thinking in authentic, age-appropriate contexts.

plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Scientists follow a method to discover new things.

LINES OF INQUIRY: The scientific process; Observing my world; Drawing conclusions

KEY CONCEPTS: Perspective, connection, form

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Video - Scientist's role (Week 1-3): As a class, brainstorming what a scientist does, what sort of things a scientist works with, and what things a scientist can discover. Exploring picture story books about rock collections, nature, and parks.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Nature Detective Walk (Week 4): Read 'The Tiny Explorers', go outside with magnifying glasses and clipboards to observe plants, insects, and other living things, record findings with drawings and writing, sort and group creatures or plants by features.
- Weather Watchers (Week 5): Daily weather observations, drawing a "weather picture" each morning, sequencing pictures, discussing patterns, comparing with weather in another place via webcam or online weather reports.
- Ice Melting Challenge (Week 6): Groups receive ice cubes and tools (bowl, spoon, salt, warm water), predict and test fastest ways to melt ice, record results in a chart.
- Rolling Ramp Investigation (Week 7): Use ramps and objects (balls, toy cars, cylinders), predict which will roll farthest, test and measure, discuss factors affecting movement.
- Mystery Materials Bag (Week 8): Explore properties of materials (fabric, plastic, metal, sponge, wood) by touch, describe and identify, discuss uses.

ASSESSMENT: Student work samples (observation drawings, weather chart, written responses), sharing at Student Led conferences about the nature walk experience, class discussions, and presentations of findings.

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Action opportunities include building on prior learning and supporting student-initiated action, such as participation, lifestyle choices, and advocacy.
- Chart of a Scientist on display to capture initial thinking.
- Transdisciplinary connections: Written responses in English, science experiments, nature walk experience, music (exploring rhythm patterns and instrumental timbres from past and present), and opportunities for digital skills integration (e.g., using webcams for weather comparison).
- Outdoor and sensory learning are emphasized to boost engagement and support different learning styles.
- Opportunities for students to receive teacher and peer feedback are embedded throughout the unit.
- To improve: involve students more in self-assessment and reflection to increase awareness of their own learning process.

ci: Living things share our world and have an important role in nature
lo: The different living things around us; The roles living things play in nature; How humans and other living things share space
plannerText: Not yet documented in PDF.

ci: Relationships impact our wellbeing.
lo: Developing relationships; How those around us impact our behaviours and interactions; Keeping ourselves healthy and safe

s.d: Students create and perform short puppet shows using Puppet Pals to act out friendship scenarios that demonstrate how relationships impact wellbeing. They design characters and settings that show sharing, helping, and caring, then record their voices to bring their stories to life, highlighting positive behaviours and emotions in friendships.

Students compose and record original soundtracks in GarageBand to represent different emotions and relationship scenarios. They experiment with tempo, instruments, and sound effects to express feelings like happiness, frustration, or calm, then share their audio creations as part of class discussions about wellbeing and friendship.

Students create a digital portfolio on Seesaw where they draw pictures of themselves with friends and record short voice messages explaining how these relationships make them feel and why they are important for their wellbeing. Teachers guide learners to share and listen to each other's stories, fostering a supportive classroom community that reflects the unit?s central idea about relationships impacting wellbeing.

Students create vibrant visual collages in PicCollage that illustrate 'What friendship looks like at school,' using photos, stickers, and emojis to represent acts of kindness, teamwork, and support. They build their collages to reflect the unit?s central idea that relationships impact our wellbeing, explicitly connecting to developing friendships and how those around us influence our behaviours. Learners then present their collages to the class as conversation starters, fostering discussion about positive interactions and keeping ourselves healthy and safe.

Guide students to code simple animations in ScratchJr that show two characters navigating a playground scenario?such as sharing, resolving a conflict, or inviting someone to play?demonstrating how positive relationships impact wellbeing. Students can narrate their stories and share them with peers.

Students work in small groups to design and build a miniature playground using recycled materials, cardboard, popsticks, and clay, with each playground area representing a different feeling or positive social interaction (e.g., 'Kindness Slide', 'Sharing Sandbox', 'Helping Hand Swing'). Once constructed, students use Sphero Indi cars decorated as 'Friend Bots' to navigate the playground, coding simple colour paths that represent different friendship scenarios (e.g., Indi drives to the 'Kindness Slide' when students code a path for helping a friend). The project follows the Design Cycle: students empathise by discussing playground experiences, ideate playground zones for positive relationships, prototype and build, test their Indi's journey, and reflect on how these spaces and actions foster wellbeing and friendships.

plannerText: The Prep 'Who We Are' unit explores how relationships impact wellbeing, focusing on developing friendships, understanding how those around us influence our behaviours, and recognising balance in our interactions. Key activities include read-alouds, oral discussions, drawing emotions, role-play, and creating an essential agreement to foster positive classroom relationships.

plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Relationships impact our wellbeing.

LINES OF INQUIRY: Developing relationships; How those around us impact our behaviours and interactions; Recognising our balance

KEY CONCEPTS: Function, causation, change

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Read alouds that explore the lines of inquiry, followed by oral discussions and brainstorming as a whole class in response. Specific activities include creating an Essential Agreement as a class, and reading "How Do Dinosaurs Play with Their Friends?" by Jane Yolen and Mark Teague, with students discussing and sharing what makes a good friend.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Essential Agreement creation as a class
- Read alouds: "How Do Dinosaurs Play with Their Friends?" (Jane Yolen & Mark Teague), "The Feelings Book" (Todd Parr), "Have You Filled a Bucket Today?" (Carol McCloud), "Should I Share My Ice Cream?" (Mo Willems)
- Oral discussions about friendship, feelings, and kindness
- Drawing activities: students draw how a friend can support them when feeling different emotions
- Pair work: practicing sharing and taking turns with materials (e.g., building a tower with blocks)
- Activity: Creating actions through photo form to represent Learner Profile attributes
- Visual Art: Self-portraits focusing on identity and emotions, drawing faces with different emotions, responding to music with artwork expressing emotions
- PSPE: Activities focused on cooperation, emotional expression, body awareness, personal space, and turn-taking in PE
- Portfolio task reflecting on play, enjoyment, and movement's effect on body and emotions

ASSESSMENT: 
- Portfolio task where students reflect on what they enjoy, how they play with others, and how movement affects their body and emotions
- Evidence from group games, role-play, drawings, and oral discussions
- Ongoing teacher observation of social skills and application of learning in classroom and play situations

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Local connections focus on students transitioning to school life and navigating classroom/playground dynamics
- Oral discussions used to assess prior knowledge about positive relationships and friendship
- Collaboration with specialists (e.g., PE, Visual Art) is noted as an area for further development
- Visual Art and PSPE are integrated to support identity, emotions, and wellbeing
- Technology: Students could use digital tools to document their learning (e.g., photos of actions representing Learner Profile attributes, digital portfolios)
- Adjustments made for class size and dynamics; activities adapted to student needs and readiness
- Emphasis on student agency: co-constructing Essential Agreement, choice in how to show learning (drawing, role-play, discussion)
- The unit is designed to support transition to school, build friendships, and establish classroom expectations for a positive learning environment

ci: Emotions can be expressed in different ways.
lo: Recognising our own and others' emotions; Responding to and interpreting our emotions; Different ways we express emotions

s.d: Students unleash their creativity by drawing expressive self-portraits and vibrant emotion masks in Sketchbook, using digital brushes and colors to capture feelings like joy, sadness, and surprise. As they experiment with layering and blending, learners deepen their understanding of how emotions can be visually communicated, bringing their artworks to life in a dynamic, interactive gallery.

Students document and reflect on their own emotional expressions by uploading photos, drawings, or short videos showing different feelings. Teachers can prompt students to capture moments throughout the day when they feel happy, sad, excited, or calm, and add voice recordings to explain their emotions.

Students create their own short podcasts where they describe different emotions and share personal stories about times they felt happy, sad, or excited. Using Adobe Express Podcasting, they practise speaking clearly and listening carefully, building empathy as they hear classmates? voices and feelings come alive through sound. This engaging activity deepens understanding of how emotions can be expressed through tone and words, not just pictures or actions. https://podcast.adobe.com

Students use Puppet Pals to create short animated skits where characters experience and express different emotions in various scenarios (e.g., losing a toy, making a new friend). This helps students practice recognising and communicating emotions through storytelling and role-play.

Students create visual emotion collages using PicCollage, combining photos of their own facial expressions, emojis, and colourful backgrounds to represent a range of feelings. They can label each collage with the emotion and share with the class, sparking discussion about how we show what we feel.

Students work in teams to design and build expressive 'emotion robots' using Sphero Indi cars as the moving base. First, they brainstorm and sketch how to represent different feelings (happy, sad, excited, etc.) through facial features, colors, and accessories. Then, they use cardboard, recycled materials, felt, googly eyes, and craft supplies to physically build and decorate 3D robot 'faces' and bodies that attach securely to the Sphero Indi. Each team codes simple color paths for their robot to follow, choreographing a 'parade' that expresses a sequence of emotions (e.g., Indi moves along a blue path for 'sad', then a yellow path for 'happy'). The project culminates in a class robot parade, with students explaining their design choices and how their robots' movements and appearance express different emotions, connecting hands-on making, coding, and social-emotional learning.

plannerText: This unit explores how emotions can be expressed in different ways, with students engaging in activities such as reading picture books about feelings, creating artworks and masks to represent emotions, participating in role-play and music to communicate feelings, and reflecting on their own and others' emotional expressions through group discussions and visual projects.

The project culminates in a class robot parade, with students explaining their design choices and how their robots' movements and appearance express different emotions, connecting hands-on making, coding, and social-emotional learning.","url":""}]
plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Emotions can be expressed in different ways.

LINES OF INQUIRY: Recognising our own and others' emotions; Responding to and interpreting our emotions; Communicating our emotions

KEY CONCEPTS: Perspective, causation

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Write the word 'feelings' on a big classroom chart. Use think, pair, share to build instructional routine for sharing thinking. Students turn and talk about the word feelings and what it means with a talking buddy. Teacher records ideas heard as students talk with a partner. Students work in four groups to record their thinking about feelings using words, drawings or sentences. Questions from this activity are added to the JS Wonder Wall.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Read aloud and discuss picture story books connecting to emotions (e.g., You are Brave by Margaret O'Hair, I am Peace by Susan Verde, In My Heart by Jo Witek).
- Shared reading comprehension using "My Many Coloured Days" with visualising and drawing thoughts as the text is read.
- Self-assessment rubric and See, Think, Wonder routines with feelings faces.
- Creative activity: My Many Coloured Days ? creative feelings people.
- Group collage depicting an emotion, including a photograph of the group showing this emotion, focusing on colour associations.
- Individual mask-making to show an emotion, with group guessing games.
- Role-play activities to express emotions through body language and facial expressions.
- Music: Exploring how music can express emotions (tempo, pitch, timbre, dynamics) and creating a soundscape to accompany a book.
- PSPE: Health lessons on recognising and communicating emotions respectfully; PE Teamwork Challenge to express feelings in collaborative settings.
- Wellbeing and Social Thinking curriculum connections.
- Ongoing use of the JS Wonder Wall for student questions and wonderings.

ASSESSMENT: 
- Self-assessment rubric (T1, W9)
- See, Think, Wonder charts
- Creative feelings people activity
- Group and individual art projects (collage, masks)
- Student participation in discussions and role-plays
- Portfolio tasks (e.g., Communicating Feelings health task, PE Teamwork Challenge)
- Music soundscape recordings (noted as not included in final book recording)
- Sharing learning with the community

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Strong integration with Wellbeing, Social Thinking, and Health curriculum.
- Transdisciplinary connections with English (literature and vocabulary), Visual Art (colour and emotion), Music (soundscapes), and PSPE (emotional regulation in health and PE).
- Opportunities for student agency through choice in art and reflection activities.
- Use of digital tools not specified, but opportunities exist for digital portfolios (e.g., SeeSaw), recording soundscapes, and documenting learning with photographs.
- Teacher reflection notes highlight the value of peer reflection, structured self-assessment, and the importance of integrating emotional literacy across subjects.
- Next steps include more structured peer reflection and ensuring music recordings are included in final presentations.

ci: Services are established to help the needs of people in the community.
lo: Local services; Why we need local services in our community; How services meet personal and community needs

s.d: Students create simple puppet shows using Puppet Pals to role-play different community helpers and the services they provide. They design characters representing local service workers, then record their voices explaining how these services meet personal and community needs, making their understanding visible and engaging for classmates.

Learners document a 'Day in the Life' of a chosen community helper by capturing photos or short videos using the iPad camera, then annotating with voice recordings and drawings in Seesaw. This creates a multimedia portfolio that showcases their understanding of the roles and importance of local services.

Students collaborate to create a short class video titled 'Our Community Helpers' where they record and edit clips of themselves describing different local services and how these services support the community. Using iMovie?s simple editing tools, they add photos, voice recordings, and captions to bring their presentations to life, reinforcing the central idea that services help meet people?s needs.

Children take photos of community helper puppets or drawings, then use ChatterPix Kids to animate the images and record short messages as if spoken by the helpers. This brings the roles of community workers to life and encourages empathy and communication skills.

Students go on a 'Community Services Scavenger Hunt' around the school or local area, taking photos with iPads of people and places that provide services. They then create vibrant collages in PicCollage, labeling each service and sharing their findings with the class.

Students work in teams to design and build a miniature 'community' using recycled materials, cardboard, and craft supplies, creating key locations like a fire station, hospital, and post office. Each team chooses a community helper (e.g., firefighter, paramedic, postal worker) and invents a rescue or service scenario (e.g., delivering medicine, putting out a small fire). Using Bee-Bots with custom-decorated shells matching their helper, students program routes to navigate their helpers through the physical town, overcoming crafted obstacles and delivering aid. The project follows the Design Cycle: students empathise with community needs, define a helper's mission, ideate and build the town, prototype and test Bee-Bot routes, and refine their designs for a final demonstration to the class.

plannerText: In this unit, Year 1 students explore how services are established to help meet the needs of people in the community. Through read-alouds, guest speakers, research, and presentations, students investigate local services, their roles, and how they support the community. Learning experiences include interviews, creative presentations, and writing appreciation letters to community helpers.

plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Services are established to help the needs of people in the community.

LINES OF INQUIRY: Local services; Why we need local services in our community; How services meet personal and local community needs

KEY CONCEPTS: Form, causation, function

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Moving to Mars! If we started a new community on Mars, what services would we need? Students discuss and record their wonderings on a Wonder Wall. Students work as a class to design a community on butcher's paper.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Students complete a mini-book about community helpers.
- Carousel activity: students record their ideas about needs, wants, services, and goods; unpack what a service is.
- Guest speaker visits: Nurse Simone and a doctor (cardiologist, parent).
- School community interviews: students create interview questions for local community service people.
- Learning walk: exploring the school community and its services.
- Ongoing reading lessons with community helper texts, vocabulary building, and comprehension.
- Incursion with the Royal Flying Doctor Service.
- Students choose a community helper to write an information report about.
- Home learning: students prepare a presentation about a chosen community helper and present findings to the class.
- Students write a letter to local 'heroes' (community workers).
- Use of digital resources: Get Epic! for research, PowerPoint presentations for viewing and presenting tasks.
- Library sessions focusing on services such as Libraries, Outback services, Surf Lifesavers, Service Animals, supported by specific PowerPoint resources and books.

ASSESSMENT: 
- Anecdotal assessment and observation of student participation and wonderings.
- Student presentations about a chosen community helper.
- Community helper information report.
- Letters to local community workers.
- SeeSaw task for sharing learning with the community.

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Strong integration with literacy (reading nonfiction texts, writing reports, presentations).
- Collaboration with Library for aligned read-alouds and resource support.
- Use of digital tools (Get Epic!, PowerPoint, SeeSaw) for research, presentation, and portfolio documentation.
- Guest speakers and real-world connections significantly enhance engagement.
- Opportunities for student agency through choice of community helper and presentation format.
- Future suggestions: incorporate more hands-on or role-playing experiences, and provide more structured support for formulating questions and presentations.
- Transdisciplinary connections with social studies, literacy, and inquiry skills; some integration with Chinese (research and presentation skills).
- Assessment includes both formative (anecdotal, observation) and summative (presentations, reports, letters) evidence.

ci: Patterns help us to interpret the world around us.
lo: Patterns and cycles in our lives; Different patterns around us; How patterns can be created, interpreted and extended

s.d: Students use Sphero Indi to design and navigate pattern mazes that represent natural cycles, such as the water cycle or life cycles. By programming color-coded paths, they physically model and explain how patterns repeat in the world around us, reinforcing their understanding through hands-on exploration.

Students create talking pattern creatures by drawing or photographing patterns (e.g., butterfly wings, water cycle stages), then use ChatterPix Kids to animate them with their own voices explaining the pattern or cycle. This brings abstract concepts to life in a playful, memorable way.

Facilitate a digital pattern journal in Seesaw where students document daily or weekly observations of patterns and cycles (e.g., weather, classroom routines, plant growth), using photos, voice recordings, and drawings to reflect on how these patterns help us interpret the world.

Students create vibrant digital collages that showcase different patterns and cycles they observe in their daily lives and the natural world. They combine photos, drawings, and text to illustrate concepts like the life cycle of a butterfly or repeating shapes in nature, making their learning visual and interactive. This hands-on activity helps them interpret and extend patterns while practicing digital creativity and storytelling.

Students design simple animations in ScratchJr to model and extend repeating patterns or cycles (e.g., day-night, seasons, animal life cycles). They can program characters to move, change, or transform in a sequence, visually demonstrating their understanding of how patterns repeat and change.

Students work in teams to design and build a large, 3D diorama representing the four seasons or a life cycle (e.g., butterfly, frog) using cardboard, clay, fabric scraps, and recycled materials. They incorporate simple mechanisms (e.g., rotating discs, sliders, pop-up elements) to show changes over time. Using Bee-Bots, students code simple paths that travel through their diorama, triggering physical events (like a flower blooming or a sun rising) at each station. Teams use iPads to record short videos explaining the patterns and cycles demonstrated in their interactive model, blending creative building, basic robotics, and digital storytelling.

plannerText: This unit immerses Year 1 students in exploring patterns and cycles in the natural world and daily life, including life cycles, seasons, and the water cycle. Students engage in hands-on investigations, digital research, and creative activities to observe, document, and interpret patterns, building critical thinking and research skills while making connections across science, art, and literacy.

plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Patterns helps us to interpret the world around us.

LINES OF INQUIRY: Patterns and cycles in our lives; Different patterns around us; How patterns can be created, interpreted and extended

KEY CONCEPTS: Change, function, perspective

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Provocation of unveiling the butterfly chrysalis ? students are asked what they think is underneath the cloth, if they can think of another animal that changes, and what they think will happen to the chrysalis. Chrysalis kit is placed in the classroom for 3 weeks, and each morning students observe changes.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Research skills using Get Epic to choose a life cycle of interest and demonstrate understanding in writing
- Technology use: iPad and Get Epic app for research
- Read aloud texts exploring information and vocabulary about different cycles
- Paraphrasing information about animal life-cycles using templates
- Exploring different types of patterns and cycles (animal life cycles, plant life-cycles, water cycle, seasons) through texts and videos
- Home learning task: students choose and research a cycle of personal interest
- Local exploration: taking photos of objects with magnifying glass lens iPad and observing patterns in the local community
- Visual Art: drawing patterns found in nature and buildings
- Design Technologies: making a model of a butterfly's lifecycle
- Digital Portfolio: SeeSaw
- Music: Watching videos about different life-cycles (YouTube Channel ? Pants Bear)
- Chinese: Learning patterns in the days of the week through "The Very Hungry Caterpillar" story and narrating favourite parts in Chinese

ASSESSMENT: 
- Written responses to research tasks
- Paraphrasing information using templates
- Home learning research project and presentation
- Digital Portfolio (SeeSaw)
- Observations and anecdotal assessment of participation and understanding
- Butterfly journal observing changes to chrysalises

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Action opportunities include participation, lifestyle choices, and advocacy (e.g., UN Sustainable Development Goals)
- Transdisciplinary connections: Literacy (read alouds, writing), Maths (measurement ? time, seasons), Science (posing questions, making observations, comparing predictions), Social Studies (collecting and recording information), Visual Art, PE (emotional development linked to perspective and change), Chinese (patterns in language)
- Digital skills: Use of Epic for research, SeeSaw for digital portfolio, iPad for observation and documentation
- Student agency supported through choice in research topics and home learning tasks
- Integration with Australian Curriculum and cross-curriculum priorities (Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Histories and Cultures, Asia and Australia's Engagement with Asia, Sustainability)
- Opportunities for further exploration in Art and Maths patterns noted for future units
- Students particularly enjoyed hands-on experiences (chrysalis kit) and expressed interest in observing other animal life-cycles (e.g., chicks hatching)

ci: Plants are a life sustaining resource.
lo: Products derived from plants; Plants all around us; The interconnectedness of plants

s.d: Learners program Sphero Indi cars to travel through a garden-themed obstacle course, representing the journey of a plant from seed to flower. As they navigate each stage, students explain plant needs and life cycle changes, blending coding with science and storytelling.

Students become young botanists by recording their own short podcasts explaining how different plants help sustain life, using simple language and sounds from nature they capture around the school garden. They create engaging audio stories that share their observations of plant life cycles and the important roles plants play in our world, building confidence in speaking and listening while connecting deeply to the unit?s central idea. Explore Adobe Express Podcasting at https://podcast.adobe.com for a seamless, age-appropriate audio creation experience.

Students create a simple class video documenting the stages of a plant?s life cycle by filming their own drawings, plant observations, and short explanations. They use iMovie to arrange clips in sequence, add voice narration, and include photos of plants they find around the school, producing a multimedia story that shows how plants are a life-sustaining resource.

Guide students to collect leaves, petals, or seeds, photograph them with the iPad, and create digital plant collages in PicCollage. They then upload their creations to Seesaw, adding voice reflections about how each part helps plants sustain life, fostering both creativity and scientific thinking.

Students design simple animations showing the journey of a seed growing into a plant, including the resources it needs (sun, water, soil). This visual coding activity helps solidify understanding of plant life cycles and the concept of plants as life-sustaining resources.

Students work in teams to design and construct a large, 3D garden model using recycled cardboard, fabric scraps, bottle caps, and clay to represent different stages of plant growth (seed, sprout, mature plant, flower, fruit). They embed simple circuits (using battery packs, LED lights, and copper tape) to illuminate parts of the garden that represent key life stages, such as a glowing seed or a blooming flower. Using iPads, students record short audio explanations for each stage and attach them to their models with QR codes (generated with a QR code app). When scanned, these codes play the students' own voices explaining what happens at each stage. The project follows the Design Cycle: students investigate plant needs, brainstorm and sketch designs, build prototypes, test the circuits and QR codes, and share their interactive garden with peers and families.

plannerText: This unit immerses Year 1 students in exploring how plants are a life-sustaining resource. Through hands-on investigations, digital research, and creative projects, students observe plant life cycles, document changes, and communicate their understanding using visual and tactile digital tools. The learning is grounded in real-world observations and student agency, with strong links to science, literacy, and transdisciplinary inquiry.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Homes reflect our culture and environment.
lo: Different types of homes; Characteristics of a home; The importance of sustainable living spaces

s.d: Students collaborate to create short audio tours describing different types of homes from around the world, sharing their observations about how homes reflect culture and environment. Using Adobe Express Podcasting, they record their voices narrating key features and stories, then listen to and reflect on each other's work to deepen their understanding of sustainable living spaces.

Invite students to draw or photograph different types of homes (e.g., yurts, igloos, apartments) and use ChatterPix Kids to animate the homes, giving them a 'voice' to share facts about their culture, environment, and sustainability features in a fun, accessible way.

Students collaborate to create a short class video titled 'Homes Around the World' by filming and narrating their own mini home models and drawings, capturing key features that reflect culture and environment. They use iMovie to arrange clips, add simple voiceovers, and include text labels, developing their storytelling and digital literacy skills while showcasing their understanding of sustainable living spaces.

Facilitate a 'Home Design Reflection Journal' on Seesaw, where students document their learning journey by uploading photos of their mini home builds, voice-recording their design choices, and responding to prompts about how their home reflects their culture or environment.

Have students create vibrant visual collages comparing two types of homes (e.g., a houseboat and a cave home) using PicCollage, incorporating images, stickers, and simple text to highlight similarities and differences in structure, materials, and cultural significance.

Students work in teams to research and select a type of home from a different culture or environment (e.g., igloo, stilt house, mud hut, yurt). Using recycled materials, cardboard, fabric, and clay, they build detailed mini models of these homes, ensuring features reflect their chosen culture and climate. Next, students program Sphero Indi cars with colour tiles or Bee-Bots with directional cards to 'visit' each home, creating a physical map with paths connecting the models. As the robots travel, students narrate or record short facts about each home using iPads, which classmates can listen to at each stop. This hands-on project integrates design, building, basic robotics, and cultural storytelling, culminating in an interactive showcase where peers and families can explore the village and learn about global diversity.

plannerText: In this unit, Year 1 students explore how homes reflect culture and environment by investigating a variety of global home types, building mini models, engaging in comparative discussions, and showcasing their learning through creative projects and a learning showcase. The unit emphasizes hands-on inquiry, cultural appreciation, and student agency through open-ended building and reflection tasks.

plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Homes reflect our culture and environment.

LINES OF INQUIRY: Different types of homes; Characteristics of a home; The importance of sustainability

KEY CONCEPTS: Connection and Form

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Students use craft materials to make a classroom display of houses. Writing task: Write what you love about your home. Read Epic texts "Homes Around the World" and "Homes Then and Now".

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Reading information texts about different types of homes (e.g., Gunya, Queenslander, houseboat, igloo, yurt, apartment, etc.)
- Watching informative videos about different types of homes
- Venn diagram comparing two types of homes
- Building mini houses (Design Technologies)
- Observational drawings of houses from around the world in Visual Art; select a house and create it using different mediums
- Exploring Epic to research facts about different types of houses
- Writing tasks: paraphrasing facts, Venn diagrams, and writing about what students love about their own home
- Learning Showcase where students present their mini homes and learning
- Dance & Drama: Exploring connection and form through homes and environments, discovering non-human personifications
- Chinese: Learning about Chinese characters, comparing past (oracle bone script) and modern forms

ASSESSMENT: Building mini homes; Learning Showcase; Writing tasks (what do you love about your home, paraphrasing facts, Venn diagrams); Digital Portfolio ? SeeSaw

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Action opportunities include building mini homes and presenting at the Learning Showcase.
- Students are encouraged to research and present about homes from different cultures, supporting open-mindedness and international mindedness.
- Digital skills are integrated through research on Epic and documentation in SeeSaw.
- Design Technologies is used for building mini houses.
- Transdisciplinary connections are made with Social Studies (comparing past and present homes, considering sustainability), English (reading and writing tasks), Visual Art, and Chinese.
- The unit encourages student agency and creativity, with open-ended tasks and opportunities for students to choose how they present their learning (slides, videos, posters, etc.).
- Teacher reflection notes suggest that the open-ended nature of the home-building project fostered creativity and ownership, and that literacy was linked weekly in a transdisciplinary way. There is a suggestion to explore measurement in future iterations of the unit.

ci: The actions we take influence our safety and wellbeing.
lo: Safe environments; How our actions affect ourselves and others; Keeping our body and mind healthy

s.d: Students create a 'Healthy Habits Hero' by drawing themselves or a character making a safe or healthy choice (e.g., washing hands, wearing a helmet). Using ChatterPix Kids, they animate the character to give a spoken safety or wellbeing tip, then upload their video to Seesaw for a class 'Wellbeing Gallery'. This app-smash empowers student voice and makes health messages memorable and fun.

Students create a vibrant class video where they act out and narrate healthy habits that keep their bodies and minds safe and well. Using iMovie, they combine their recordings with colourful images and simple animations to produce a lively, informative film that showcases how their daily actions influence wellbeing. This collaborative project brings the central idea to life through storytelling and multimedia expression.

Students design obstacle courses for Sphero Indi cars that represent healthy and unhealthy choices in daily routines. As they navigate the course, they explain how different actions?like eating well or exercising?help keep our bodies safe and well, making abstract concepts tangible and fun.

Learners go on a 'Safety Scavenger Hunt' around the school, using the iPad camera to capture examples of safe and unsafe practices (e.g., tidy vs. messy walkways, correct vs. incorrect handwashing). They assemble their photos into a PicCollage poster, labeling each with emojis or simple icons to reinforce visual learning.

Students design simple animations in ScratchJr where characters make choices that affect their wellbeing (e.g., choosing to eat fruit, wear sunscreen, or take a break when tired). They can program cause-and-effect sequences, deepening their understanding of how actions influence outcomes.

Students work in teams to design and build a life-sized human body outline using cardboard, fabric scraps, recycled containers, and craft materials. They create removable, tactile 'organ' pieces (e.g., heart, lungs, stomach) using clay, felt, and other textured supplies. Each organ is fitted with a simple conductive material (aluminum foil, copper tape) and connected to a Makey Makey kit and iPad running a pre-made interactive soundboard (e.g., GarageBand or a recorded Keynote). When an organ is placed correctly, it completes a circuit and triggers a sound or student-recorded message about how that body part contributes to safety and wellbeing (e.g., 'The heart keeps your blood moving!'). Students follow the Design Cycle: empathize (explore their own bodies and healthy choices), define (choose key organs and messages), ideate (sketch and plan), prototype (build and wire organs), test (try with classmates), and share (present their interactive body board at the Healthy Bodies Museum).

plannerText: This unit explores how the actions we take influence our safety and wellbeing, with students engaging in activities such as daily read-alouds about the human body, building life-size body models, participating in healthy habits inquiry stations, and creating informative books about body parts and healthy choices. The learning is highly hands-on and visual, connecting to real-life experiences like the swimming program and culminating in student-led presentations and a Healthy Bodies Museum.
