plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: The actions we take influence our safety and wellbeing

LINES OF INQUIRY: How the human body grows and changes; Ways we can keep our body and mind healthy; Safe environments

KEY CONCEPTS: Function

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Class brainstorm and "body outline" activity?students label known parts on a blank human body template to elicit prior knowledge and curiosities about the human body. Follow-up includes developing wonder questions to guide the inquiry wall.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Daily interactive read-alouds of nonfiction texts (e.g., See Inside Your Body, The Human Body series, AI generated texts), with post-it notes for "new learning" added to the inquiry wall.
- Incursion: Mad About Science ? The Human Body, with hands-on exploration of the eye, respiratory system, and digestive system.
- Mini-lessons on nonfiction text features; students draft, illustrate, and publish their own Informative Book about the body and healthy habits, shared at Student-Led Conferences (SLCs).
- Healthy Habits Inquiry Stations: Nutrition sorting, exercise/heart rate exploration, hygiene role-play, sleep/rest charting.
- Life-Size Body Models: In pairs, students create organs and body systems, then assemble them on life-size body outlines.
- Life-Size Skeletons: Trace each child's body, introduce bones and the skeletal system, and assemble a life-size skeleton.
- Quiz Book for SLCs: Students generate quiz questions about body systems and healthy living, design a quiz book, and present at SLCs.
- Healthy Habits Presentations: Students create a presentation about their healthy habits using a multi-modal element (PowerPoint, booklet, poster, video, or model).
- Buddy Reading during Book Week: Students share their Human Body Book with Year 5s.

ASSESSMENT: 
- Informative writing (My Human Body Book) assessed for nonfiction text features and scientific understanding.
- Quiz Book for Student-Led Conferences as evidence of recall and application.
- Healthy Habits presentations (multi-modal).
- Anecdotal assessment during inquiry stations and hands-on activities.
- Sharing learning with families at SLCs and with Year 5 buddies.

TEACHER NOTES: 
- Strong integration with literacy (read-alouds, informative writing), science (body systems, health), and PE (physical development, safe movement choices).
- Inquiry wall and wonder wall used for student questions and new learning.
- Digital skills: Opportunity for guided use of digital resources or library texts for student-led research.
- Students encouraged to use multi-modal presentations (PowerPoint, video, poster, model).
- Transdisciplinary connections with Music (Vivaldi's Four Seasons for composition), Visual Art (not specified), and PE (movement, teamwork, wellbeing).
- Action opportunities: Making healthy choices, reflecting on routines, presenting to peers and families.
- Teacher reflection notes suggest more structured formative checkpoints and more student-led research in future iterations.

ci: Artistic appreciation and expressions inspire our imagination and creativity.
lo: Forms of expression; Expressing thoughts and ideas; Understanding and appreciating art

s.d: Students collaborate to script and record short podcasts where they describe their own artistic creations and explain how their art, poetry, or music expresses their thoughts and feelings. They listen to each other's recordings to appreciate diverse forms of expression and reflect on how creativity inspires imagination within the class community.

Students photograph their own artworks or creative projects, then use ChatterPix Kids to animate their creations and give them a 'voice' to explain their inspiration and artistic choices. These talking artworks are then compiled into a digital class gallery using Book Creator, celebrating diverse forms of expression and imagination.

Learners document their creative process by capturing photos and videos of their artwork, poetry, and music explorations in Seesaw. They use drawing and voice annotation tools to reflect on how their imagination and appreciation of others' work has grown, building a rich, multimedia portfolio of their artistic journey.

Students create vibrant digital collages that showcase different forms of artistic expression such as drawings, photos, and words inspired by their exploration of art, poetry, music, and dance. They combine their own artwork and images from the classroom to visually express how creativity and imagination can be communicated in unique ways, reflecting the unit?s central idea of artistic appreciation inspiring creativity.

Students create and record original short plays or fairy tales using Puppet Pals on iPads, focusing on expressing different emotions and perspectives through their characters. They produce a digital puppet show that demonstrates their understanding of artistic expression and storytelling, connecting directly to the unit?s central idea about how artistic appreciation inspires imagination and creativity. This activity encourages students to explore forms of expression and share their creative stories with the class, deepening their appreciation for diverse ways to communicate ideas and feelings.

s.t: Emotion Machines: Build a Moving Sculpture
s.d: Students work in teams to design and build a kinetic 'Emotion Machine'?a moving sculpture that visually represents a chosen feeling (e.g., joy, surprise, calm) using cardboard, recycled materials, pipe cleaners, fabric, and craft supplies. They use simple robotics kits (e.g., Sphero Indi or basic motors with battery packs) to add motion or light to their sculpture, such as spinning parts, waving arms, or glowing sections. The project follows the Design Cycle: students empathise by discussing how different emotions look and feel, ideate creative ways to express these through movement and materials, prototype their machines, test and refine the movement, and finally present their sculptures, explaining how the physical and digital elements together express their chosen emotion.

plannerText: This unit explores how artistic appreciation and expressions inspire imagination and creativity in Year 2 students. Learners engage with diverse forms of expression?including art, poetry, and music?through hands-on activities, collaborative projects, and reflection, building empathy, risk-taking, and appreciation for their own and others' creativity.
humanVerifiedResetReason: Suggestion edited, swapped, regenerated or changed after human verification
plannerContextRich:
CENTRAL IDEA: Artistic appreciation and expressions inspire our imagination and creativity

LINES OF INQUIRY: Forms of expression; Expressing thoughts and ideas; Understanding and appreciating other's creativity and imagination

KEY CONCEPTS: Form, Perspective, Communication

PROVOCATION / TUNING IN: Bus Stop Exploration in two classrooms. In Jane's room: students explored books from the library collection and artefacts such as art pieces, photos, and instruments. In Alex's room: students discussed what creativity means to them and watched ClickView short clips linked to artistic expression. Students completed a "Unit of Inquiry Detective" template and a See, Think, Wonder sheet (though this was found to be challenging for Year 2 learners). There was also an "I am poem" activity, where students typed their poem in Book Creator and published an artwork with watercolour paint.

LEARNING ENGAGEMENTS: 
- Poetry writing in pairs
- Learner Profile Caring Detective activities
- Essential Agreement reflections
- "Size of the problem" and "Zones of Regulation" wellbeing activities
- "Beautiful Oops" exploration
- Exploring Art and Music forms as 'data collectors' using frayer models, tables, and labelled diagrams
- Listening to different musical instruments and discussing subject-specific vocabulary
- Creating pet portraits combining knowledge of various art styles and skills learnt in Art lessons with Popi Roumeliotous
- Producing several pieces of Art and exploring composition through Music apps such as Chrome Music Lab and Blob Orchestra
- Be Balanced choice boards (guided meditations, drawing tutorials, reading about the Arts, listening to music, watching dance performances)
- Haiku chalk poems in the Music Courtyard
- Year 2 class concerts

ASSESSMENT: 
- Ongoing production of Art pieces
- Exploration of composition through Music apps
- Haiku chalk poems published in the Music Courtyard
- Year 2 class concerts
- Home learning project provides choice and agency
- Students co-constructed learning goals and success criteria, co-created word walls, and were involved in deciding how to display artworks and share learning on Seesaw

TEACHER NOTES: 
- The unit was highly transdisciplinary, with connections to Art, Music, Maths, and Writing.
- Students wrote poems in response to artworks and music, explored 2D and 3D shapes through art, and applied knowledge from Specialist classes.
- Students brought in materials and artefacts from home, including books connected to the Learner Profile and artworks made outside of school.
- The central idea was found to be hard for students to access and could benefit from simplification.
- Students found thinking routines challenging and needed teacher modelling and exposure.
- Students need support with organisational thinking skills and how to collect/record data; sketch noting was introduced as an option.
- Opportunities for greater collaboration with Specialist teachers are recommended for future iterations.
- Technology integration included Book Creator for poetry/art publishing, Chrome Music Lab, Blob Orchestra, and Seesaw for sharing learning.
- Students were highly engaged, especially with hands-on activities and sharing their work with the community.

ci: Marketplaces reflect the needs and wants of communities.
lo: Needs and wants; The changing needs of communities; The design process

s.d: Students use Sphero Indi to simulate a marketplace, programming their cars to travel between 'shops' and 'customers' on a classroom map. As they navigate, they discuss needs and wants, and how products move through a community, making economic concepts interactive and memorable.

Students photograph objects from home or the classroom that represent a community's needs or wants, then use ChatterPix Kids to animate these objects and record persuasive pitches explaining how their item meets changing community needs or reflects a marketplace demand. They create short, animated videos that showcase their understanding of needs, wants, and the design process, which are then shared in a class 'Marketplace Gallery' to spark discussion about value and community perspectives. This activity connects directly to the unit?s central idea by helping students explore how marketplaces respond to the evolving needs and wants of communities.

Students create individual digital portfolios on Seesaw where they document their designed products that meet specific community needs. They upload photos or drawings of their creations, record voice explanations distinguishing needs and wants, and reflect on how their designs respond to changing community needs, building a collaborative class marketplace gallery accessible to peers and families.

Facilitate a 'Market Research Collage' activity: students visit a local market (virtually or in person), take photos using iPad cameras, and use PicCollage to sort and label items as 'needs' or 'wants'. They can add stickers and voice notes to explain their reasoning, then present their findings to the class.

Guide students to create simple animated stories in ScratchJr where characters must make choices between needs and wants in a marketplace scenario. Students program their characters to interact, negotiate, and reflect on the consequences of their decisions, reinforcing the central idea through creative coding.

s.t: Marketplace Pop-Up Stalls with Interactive Robots
s.d: Students work in teams to identify a real need or want within their school community. Each team designs and builds a physical 'pop-up stall' using cardboard, recycled materials, and craft supplies to represent a marketplace booth offering their product or service. To add an interactive tech element, students program a Bee-Bot or Blue-Bot to act as a 'customer' or 'delivery robot' that visits their stall. Teams must design the booth with ramps, gates, or simple mechanisms (e.g., levers, flaps) that the robot can trigger to 'receive' a product or reveal information. The project integrates the design cycle: empathizing with community needs, ideating solutions, prototyping both the stall and interactive robot pathway, and testing with peers. This hands-on project deepens understanding of needs vs. wants and how marketplaces function, while blending digital programming with physical engineering and creativity.

plannerText: In this unit, Year 2 students explore how marketplaces reflect the needs and wants of communities, with a strong focus on distinguishing between needs and wants, understanding changing community needs, and using the design process to create solutions. Activities include role-play, sorting games, design thinking, and a culminating project where students design products to meet real community needs.

ci: Forces and motion are all around us.
lo: Forces around us; Scientific laws of motion; Innovations in technology

s.d: Learners design and test Sphero Indi obstacle courses that demonstrate different forces?like push, pull, and friction?at work. As they adjust the course and observe the car?s movement, students explain how each force affects motion, turning abstract science into hands-on discovery.

Students take photos around the school playground of real-life examples of forces (e.g., swings, slides, ramps) using the iPad Camera. They then use ChatterPix Kids to animate these objects, having them 'talk' about the forces acting on them, before uploading their creations to Seesaw for class sharing and reflection.

Students design and code simple animations or games that demonstrate push, pull, gravity, and friction. For example, they can create a scene where a character moves differently on ice versus grass, or where a ball is pushed up and down a ramp, visually exploring the effects of different forces.

Students create short digital puppet shows that demonstrate different forces and motions they have explored, such as pushing, pulling, gravity, and friction. They design characters and scenes to act out simple experiments or real-world examples, narrating the scientific ideas behind each force to deepen their understanding and share their learning in a fun, engaging way.

Students create vibrant, visual collages comparing different types of forces and simple machines. They can use photos from their playground walk, stickers, and labels to sort and classify examples of push, pull, friction, and gravity, then present their collages to the class.

Students work in teams to design and build intricate vertical marble mazes using cardboard, recycled tubes, popsticks, and connectors, incorporating ramps, levers, and pulleys to explore forces, motion, and simple machines. Using iPads with the Stop Motion Studio app, students record marbles traveling through their mazes, annotate key force interactions (push, pull, gravity, friction), and reflect on their design process. The project follows the Design Cycle: students investigate how marbles move, plan their maze layout, build and test prototypes, improve their designs based on marble performance, and present their final creations as interactive displays, blending hands-on engineering with digital documentation and analysis.

plannerText: This unit immerses Year 2 students in hands-on investigations of forces and motion, including push, pull, gravity, friction, and simple machines. Students explore these concepts through playground observations, experiments with ramps and magnets, creative projects like Rube Goldberg machines, and reflective activities that connect scientific thinking to real-world play and innovation.

ci: Taking care of our Earth starts with the choices we make.
lo: Earth's resources; How our actions affect the environment; Ways we can care for our planet

s.d: Students create short, persuasive videos using iMovie to share their learning about Earth's resources and how their choices impact the environment. They film clips of their own eco-friendly actions, such as recycling or planting, then add simple titles and voiceovers to explain ways we can care for the planet. This hands-on project empowers students to become environmental advocates by presenting their ideas in a dynamic, multimedia format.

App smash ChatterPix Kids and PicCollage: Students photograph items from their lunchboxes or classroom, use ChatterPix to make the items 'speak' about their environmental impact, and then create a visual collage advocating for better choices. This empowers students to become persuasive advocates for Earth-friendly habits in a playful, creative way.

Students input data from their 'Waste Detective' investigation into simple Excel spreadsheets, creating colourful bar graphs to compare recyclable and landfill waste. They use these visual charts to reflect on how their daily choices impact the environment and share their findings with the class to inspire collective care for the planet.

Facilitate a 'Global Earth Care Challenge' where students document daily actions that help the planet (e.g., turning off lights, composting, picking up litter) with photos and short audio reflections in Seesaw. Connect with a partner class at another Wesley campus to compare and celebrate sustainable choices.

Students create simple animated stories in ScratchJR that show how their everyday choices affect the environment, such as recycling or saving water. They design characters and scenes to demonstrate caring for Earth's resources, reinforcing the central idea through interactive digital storytelling.

Students work in teams to design and build model animal shelters using recycled materials (cardboard, fabric scraps, plastic containers) that address a specific environmental threat (e.g., heat, rain, pollution). Using Sphero Indi or Bee-Bots with simple coding, students integrate a 'smart' feature: for example, programming the robot to simulate an animal seeking shelter or triggering a sensor (like a paper flap that opens when the robot arrives). The project follows the Design Cycle: students empathize by researching local animals and their needs, ideate and sketch solutions, prototype with tangible materials, test with the robot, and reflect on how their design helps both animals and the planet. This hands-on, tech-integrated build encourages creative problem-solving and environmental stewardship.

plannerText: The Year 2 'Sharing the Planet' unit centers on the idea that caring for Earth begins with our choices. Students investigate Earth's resources, analyze how their actions impact the environment, and explore practical ways to care for the planet through hands-on inquiry, waste audits, persuasive projects, and community action.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Landmarks and places of significance tell stories about our history.
lo: The significance of places to different people and cultures; The connection between landmarks and identity; How landmarks and symbols represent our history

s.d: Students use Podcast Equipment to conduct and record short interviews with family members or community elders about local landmarks and their stories. They produce a podcast episode that captures personal memories, historical facts, and cultural significance, bringing to life the voices connected to places around Elsternwick. This audio collection helps preserve and share diverse perspectives on how landmarks tell stories about history.

Students take photos of local landmarks during a campus or neighbourhood walk using the iPad Camera app, then upload them to Seesaw. They create interactive story maps by adding voice recordings and digital drawings to explain why each place is significant and what stories it tells, making connections between place and history.

Students use PicCollage to create a vibrant collage of images, drawings, and maps representing a landmark's features and surroundings. They then use ChatterPix Kids to animate a photo of the landmark, recording a short story or fun fact from the landmark's 'perspective' to bring its history to life.

Students research a local Indigenous landmark and, using Book Creator, craft a digital picture book retelling its Dreaming story or origin. They combine their own illustrations, audio narration, and simple text, directly linking to the exploration of how places are remembered and stories are preserved.

Students watch a Clickview documentary segment about a famous Victorian landmark, then use Field Guide to Victoria to investigate its flora, fauna, and cultural stories. They create a short video or illustrated fact sheet summarising what makes the place unique and why it is important to different groups.

Students use Freeform to brainstorm and sketch ideas for a new community landmark that tells a story about their class or local area. They then use Geoboard to design the landmark's shape and layout, explaining their design choices and how the structure would help future generations remember important events or people.

plannerText: The unit 'Where We Are in Place and Time' explores how landmarks and places of significance tell stories about our history. Students investigate the meaning of significant places for different people and cultures, make connections between place and story, and learn about ways we preserve and remember historical sites through mapping, storytelling, and creative projects.

ci: Our relationships with others impact our lives.
lo: Importance of relationships; Developing and maintaining healthy relationships; The impact of relationships on wellbeing

s.d: Students use Sphero Indi to model positive relationship-building scenarios, programming the car to navigate a path representing steps in friendship or conflict resolution. As they guide Indi through each stage, learners discuss how their choices and actions impact relationships, making social-emotional learning concrete and playful.

Students take photos of themselves or classroom objects and use ChatterPix Kids to animate them, giving voice to different perspectives in a friendship scenario. For example, they can make a lunchbox 'talk' about how it feels when shared or not shared, directly linking to the central idea of relationships impacting lives.

Students create a digital journal entry in Seesaw sharing a story about a special relationship in their life, such as a friend, family member, or teacher, explaining how that relationship helps them feel happy and safe. They add drawings, voice recordings, or photos to express their feelings and reflect on how these connections support their wellbeing and help them develop healthy friendships. This activity builds empathy and self-awareness by linking personal experiences to the unit?s central idea about the impact of relationships.

Students design and act out short digital puppet shows in Puppet Pals, dramatizing real-life playground or classroom scenarios that require empathy, problem-solving, or conflict resolution. This helps them practice and reflect on healthy relationship skills in a creative, low-pressure way.

Have students create a visual 'Friendship Recipe' in PicCollage, combining photos, stickers, and drawings to represent the ingredients of a healthy relationship (e.g., kindness, listening, sharing). They can share their recipes with the class and discuss how each 'ingredient' impacts their daily interactions.

Students work in small groups to design and build miniature 'Friendship Forts' using cardboard, fabric scraps, recycled boxes, and craft materials. Each fort represents a unique community space that encourages positive relationships. Students embed simple interactive tech?such as Makey Makey kits or micro:bit touch sensors?so that touching or connecting certain parts of their fort triggers digital responses (e.g., playing a recorded message about kindness, lighting up an LED 'welcome' sign, or playing a friendship song). The project follows the Design Cycle: students empathise by discussing what makes people feel welcome and included, define features their fort should have, ideate and sketch, physically prototype, test with classmates, and iterate based on feedback. The final showcase is a 'Friendship Village', where students connect their forts with bridges or tunnels, symbolising healthy connections between people and groups.

plannerText: This year-long Year 2 unit explores how our relationships with others impact our lives, focusing on the importance of relationships, developing and maintaining healthy connections, and understanding the changeable nature of relationships. Students engage in activities that build social-emotional intelligence, empathy, and cooperative skills, supported by explicit teaching of self-regulation, friendship strategies, and reflective practices.

ci: Celebrations and traditions shape the culture of a community.
lo: The ways personal and cultural events are celebrated; Our appreciation of the diversity of cultures; How celebrations shape community identity

s.d: Students collaborate to create stop-motion animations that depict personal and cultural celebrations, using clay, paper cutouts, or small objects to illustrate traditions and community events. They plan storyboards reflecting the unit?s central idea about how celebrations shape community identity, then capture and edit their sequences to share diverse cultural stories in a creative digital format.

The teacher creates a Wise Discussion Chatbot using the Character Interview scenario and sets the bot's role as Ava, a community celebration organiser explaining why particular traditions matter to different families. The topic is ?How We Express Ourselves: Celebrations and traditions shape the culture of a community.?. Students chat with the bot to explore a named perspective through guided questioning rather than general research; for example, they ask ?How does this celebration show what your community values?? and ?What might outsiders misunderstand about this tradition??. After the chat, students produce a question bank plus a short perspective-and-evidence summary that compares the chatbot responses with unit sources linked explicitly to How We Express Ourselves.

Facilitate a collaborative digital book in Book Creator where each student authors a page about a celebration or tradition from their family or heritage, including photos, drawings, and audio explanations. The final book can be shared with the school community or families.

Guide students to document their family traditions by interviewing family members using the iPad Camera app, then upload and annotate their videos, photos, and voice notes in Seesaw. Encourage students to comment on each other's posts to foster intercultural understanding.

Challenge students to design a short, animated video or digital story in Adobe Express that explains the significance of a celebration or tradition, using music, narration, and visual effects to engage their audience.

Students work in teams to select a cultural celebration from around the world. Using recycled materials, cardboard, and craft supplies, they design and build a physical parade float that represents key symbols and traditions of their chosen celebration. Then, they integrate Sphero Indi or Bee-Bot robots by designing a coded path for the robot to 'drive' the float through a custom parade route. Students decorate the route with handmade props and signs reflecting multicultural Australia. The project follows the Design Cycle: students empathize by researching celebrations, define their float's purpose, ideate creative features, prototype with craft materials, program the robot, test the parade, and reflect on how their float expresses cultural identity and diversity.

plannerText: In this unit, students explore how celebrations and traditions shape the culture of a community by investigating personal and global cultural events, appreciating diversity, and reflecting on what it means to be a global citizen. Activities include mind mapping traditions, researching and presenting cultural identities, creating digital presentations, and collaborating on creative projects that highlight the richness of multicultural Australia.

ci: The rights and responsibilities of citizens are determined by the rules and laws of a community.
lo: The differences between rules and laws; People responsible for enforcing rules and laws; Rights and responsibilities of citizens; The consequences when rules, laws and rights are not met

s.d: Students collaboratively create interactive presentations that explore the differences between rules and laws, the roles of people who enforce them, and citizens? rights and responsibilities. They use drawing, voice recording, and annotation tools to illustrate real-life scenarios and reflect on the consequences when rules or rights are not upheld, producing a multimedia story to share with the class.

The teacher creates a Wise Discussion Chatbot using the Character Interview scenario and sets the bot's role as Ava, a community celebration organiser explaining why particular traditions matter to different families. The topic is ?How We Organise Ourselves: The rights and responsibilities of citizens are determined by the rules and laws of a community.?. Students chat with the bot to explore a named perspective through guided questioning rather than general research; for example, they ask ?How does this celebration show what your community values?? and ?What might outsiders misunderstand about this tradition??. After the chat, students produce a question bank plus a short perspective-and-evidence summary that compares the chatbot responses with unit sources linked explicitly to How We Organise Ourselves.

Students program their Micro:bit devices to create a simple digital 'rules reminder' badge that displays scrolling text messages about community rights and responsibilities. They write and test code blocks that show different rules or laws, then share their badges to explain how technology can help enforce and remind citizens of important community guidelines.

Students script and record a podcast series debating the differences between rules and laws, and the responsibilities of citizens in various global contexts. Using Adobe Express Podcasting (https://podcast.adobe.com), they interview classmates, role-play as lawmakers, and reflect on how rules shape communities, bringing real-world issues to life through engaging audio storytelling.

Students create stop-motion animations that illustrate scenarios where community rules and laws are followed or broken, showing the roles of citizens and enforcers. They storyboard their ideas, use props or drawings to represent characters, and narrate the consequences of actions to deepen understanding of rights and responsibilities in a community.

Students work in teams to design and construct a large, interactive board game that teaches the difference between rules and laws, the roles of enforcers, and the rights and responsibilities of citizens. Using recycled cardboard, craft supplies, and markers, students build a 3D game board with physical spaces, pop-up features, and hand-crafted player pieces. Integrated with tech, students program Sphero Indi or Bee-Bot robots as 'game movers', coding them to follow player turns and respond to special cards or tiles (e.g., 'Break a rule?go back 2 spaces', 'Follow a law?move forward'). Teams ideate, prototype, test, and refine their games, ensuring the mechanics reinforce citizenship concepts. The project culminates in a showcase where students teach others to play, reflecting on how their design choices represent real-world systems of rules, laws, and community responsibility.

plannerText: This unit engages students in exploring the differences between rules and laws, the people responsible for enforcing them, and the rights and responsibilities of citizens. Activities include creating persuasive texts, researching global laws, and reflecting on the consequences when rules and rights are not met. Students connect learning to local and global contexts, culminating in collaborative and creative projects that deepen their understanding of citizenship.

ci: Humans use their understanding of scientific principles to take action.
lo: The work of scientists; The process of learning; How science brings about new understandings and changes

s.d: Students create stop-motion videos that demonstrate scientific principles such as forces, energy, or material properties by animating simple experiments or models they build with everyday materials. They storyboard their ideas, capture frame-by-frame sequences, and add voice-over explanations to show how science drives innovation and action linked to real-world problems and the UN Sustainable Development Goals.

Students use Micro:bit to collect and analyse data as they investigate how humans use scientific principles to take action, connecting directly to their hands-on experiments and scientific learning. By engaging with the 'Data Handling' lesson (https://microbit.org/teach/lessons/?selected=data-handling-unit-summary), learners explore how sensors and digital tools can help them gather, interpret, and present scientific data, making their inquiry more authentic and interactive. This empowers students to see themselves as real-world scientists using technology to drive change.

The teacher creates a Wise Discussion Chatbot using the Project Ideation scenario and sets the bot's role as Leo, a student sustainability coach helping teams turn inquiry questions into realistic action projects. The topic is ?How the World Works: Humans use their understanding of scientific principles to take action.?. Students chat with the bot to test and refine possible inquiry actions or design ideas before committing to one plan; for example, they ask ?What small change could our class actually test this week?? and ?How will we know if our action helped??. After the chat, students produce a one-page project proposal naming the chosen idea, success criteria, resources, first prototype/action step and how it connects to the unit linked explicitly to How the World Works.

Students program Sphero BOLT robots to explore scientific principles like force and motion by designing obstacle courses that simulate real-world challenges. They collect data on speed, distance, and direction, then analyze how their programming decisions reflect scientific understanding and problem-solving. This hands-on activity connects directly to the central idea by showing how technology and science combine to take meaningful action.

Students use Geoboard to create and measure different shapes, exploring area and perimeter as they apply scientific measurement principles. They experiment with stretching bands to form polygons, then calculate and compare areas to understand how space and scale relate to real-world scientific problems. This hands-on activity connects directly to the unit?s focus on how scientific understanding leads to practical action.

Students will work in teams to identify a real-world environmental problem (e.g., clean water, energy, waste) linked to the UN Sustainable Development Goals. Using the Design Cycle, each team will brainstorm, sketch, and physically prototype an innovative solution using recycled materials, cardboard, popsticks, and craft supplies. To integrate technology, students will embed simple circuits (using LED bulbs, coin batteries, and conductive tape) or programmable elements (like Sphero Indi or Bee-Bots) into their models to demonstrate how their invention works. For example, a team designing a water filter might build a physical model and use LED circuits to show water flow, or a team addressing recycling could create an interactive sorting machine powered by a Sphero Indi navigating through their crafted structure. Projects will be presented in a 'Sustainable Solutions Showcase' where students explain their scientific thinking, the design process, and how their invention could make a difference in the real world.

plannerText: This unit engages Year 3 students in exploring how humans use scientific principles to take action, focusing on the work of scientists, the process of scientific learning, and how science brings about new understandings. Students conduct hands-on experiments, research scientists, create science fair projects, and connect their learning to real-world issues and the UN Sustainable Development Goals.

ci: Adaptation and sustainability ensure the survival of living things in changing environments.
lo: Adaptations and structural features of living things; Roles of living things in an ecosystem; Actions that benefit or harm ecosystems

s.d: Students create stop-motion animations that illustrate how specific animals adapt to their environments and the roles they play in ecosystems. Working in teams, they design scenes showing structural features and behaviours that help survival, then narrate their animations to explain human impacts and sustainability actions that protect these living things. This hands-on project deepens understanding of adaptation and ecosystem balance while developing storytelling and digital creativity skills.

The teacher creates a Wise Discussion Chatbot using the Project Ideation scenario and sets the bot's role as Leo, a student sustainability coach helping teams turn inquiry questions into realistic action projects. The topic is ?Sharing the Planet: Adaptation and sustainability ensure the survival of living things in changing environments.?. Students chat with the bot to test and refine possible inquiry actions or design ideas before committing to one plan; for example, they ask ?What small change could our class actually test this week?? and ?How will we know if our action helped??. After the chat, students produce a one-page project proposal naming the chosen idea, success criteria, resources, first prototype/action step and how it connects to the unit linked explicitly to Sharing the Planet.

Students use Micro:bit to create simple devices that help monitor or protect local wildlife, directly connecting to the unit's focus on adaptation and sustainability. They design and code animal trackers or alert systems, learning how technology can benefit ecosystems. (https://microbit.org/teach/lessons/?selected=protecting-animals)

Teams create a wildlife adaptation podcast, narrating stories of animal survival and human impact on ecosystems. Using Adobe Express Podcasting (https://podcast.adobe.com), students blend research, interviews, and sound effects to bring their documentaries to life, culminating in an audio showcase that highlights sustainability and adaptation.

Empower Year 3 students to become eco-explorers by designing immersive 3D virtual habitats that showcase animal adaptations and sustainable ecosystems. Using Delightex, students can collaboratively build interactive scenes where they demonstrate how living things survive and thrive in changing environments, highlight the impact of human actions, and propose creative solutions for sustainability. Their virtual worlds can be used as dynamic backdrops for their team-based documentaries and digital presentations, making their learning come alive in a visually stunning, hands-on way. Final presentations can be exported as videos or images and assembled in Canva or Adobe Express, ensuring engaging, policy-compliant sharing.

Students work in teams to design and build interactive dioramas that showcase an animal's unique adaptations for survival in a specific ecosystem. Using recycled materials, cardboard, clay, and craft supplies, groups construct detailed habitats and create movable animal models. Each diorama features at least one simple mechanical element (e.g., levers, pulleys) to demonstrate how the adaptation functions (like a chameleon's tongue or a bird's beak). Students integrate tech by embedding a simple circuit (using Makey Makey or conductive tape and micro-switches) that triggers a recorded animal fact or adaptation explanation via an iPad or laptop when a habitat feature is touched. The project follows the Design Cycle: students research, plan, prototype, test, and refine their automata, then present their interactive creations to peers, explaining the adaptation's role in sustainability and survival.

plannerText: In this unit, students investigate how adaptation and sustainability ensure the survival of living things in changing environments. They explore animal adaptations, roles in ecosystems, and human impacts, culminating in team-based documentary projects, digital presentations, and action-oriented reflections aligned with UN sustainability goals.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Exploration leads to new discoveries that can shape and change society.
lo: The factors that motivate exploration (Causation); How people reflect on discoveries from the past to inform change (Change); How exploration brings about change and new understandings for people, places and society (Change)

s.d: Students use Minecraft Education's 'Revamp Melbourne' lesson to collaboratively design a future Melbourne that responds to the needs of explorers and new settlers. They will research historical and modern discoveries that shaped the city, then build and present their own innovative city features that reflect how exploration leads to societal change. This hands-on digital build directly connects to the unit's focus on exploration, discovery, and impact on society.

Students create dynamic infographics or digital posters in Canva to showcase the motivations, journeys, and impacts of famous explorers studied in class. They can visually map push and pull factors, timelines, and the societal changes resulting from exploration, making complex historical connections accessible and engaging.

Students author interactive digital journals or historical diaries in Book Creator, writing from the perspective of a convict, explorer, or First Nations Australian during the First Fleet era. They can embed images, voice recordings, and maps to bring their narratives to life, directly linking to the unit's focus on personal histories and the impact of exploration.

Students document their research and reflections on exploration by uploading video reflections, annotated maps, and digital artifacts to Seesaw. Teachers can prompt students to compare the perspectives of different groups affected by exploration, supporting formative assessment and peer feedback.

Students use ClickView to access curated video resources about the First Fleet, global explorers, and Indigenous perspectives. They then create short video responses or digital timelines summarizing how exploration led to change, supporting critical thinking and media literacy.

s.t: Physical Makerspace Project: Mars Colony Prototype
s.d: In groups, students design and build a physical prototype of a sustainable Mars colony using recycled materials, cardboard, popsticks, and bottle caps. They must address challenges of water, food, shelter, and community rules, explicitly linking their design choices to the IB Learner Profile and UN Sustainable Development Goals. Students present their models alongside a written or digital rationale explaining how their colony reflects the lessons of past explorations and discoveries.

plannerText: This unit explores how exploration leads to new discoveries that can shape and change society, with a strong focus on the First Fleet, motivations for exploration, and the impact on both settlers and First Nations Australians. Students engage in historical research, perspective-taking, creative projects, and collaborative inquiry to understand the causes, effects, and ethical dimensions of exploration.

ci: An inquiry into the nature of the self; beliefs and values; personal, physical, mental, social and spiritual health; human relationships.
lo: Lines of inquiry to be confirmed once planner is uploaded

s.d: Students collaborate to create interactive digital presentations that explore personal strategies for resilience, growth mindset, and respectful friendships. They use drawing, voice recording, and annotation tools to illustrate real-life challenges and their responses, then reflect on how these strategies support their wellbeing and relationships. This hands-on approach deepens understanding of the central idea by making abstract social-emotional concepts visible and personal.

The teacher creates a Wise Discussion Chatbot using the Character Interview scenario and sets the bot's role as Ava, a community celebration organiser explaining why particular traditions matter to different families. The topic is ?Who We Are: An inquiry into the nature of the self; beliefs and values; personal, physical, mental, social and spiritual health; human relationships.?. Students chat with the bot to explore a named perspective through guided questioning rather than general research; for example, they ask ?How does this celebration show what your community values?? and ?What might outsiders misunderstand about this tradition??. After the chat, students produce a question bank plus a short perspective-and-evidence summary that compares the chatbot responses with unit sources linked explicitly to Who We Are.

Create interactive digital journals in Book Creator where students document real-life challenges, their emotional responses, and the strategies they used to manage change or resolve friendship fires. Include audio reflections and photos to make the journals personal and engaging.

Students design and host a Kahoot quiz game based on resilience strategies, growth vs. fixed mindset scenarios, and online/offline safety. This encourages peer teaching and reinforces key concepts from the unit in a fun, competitive format.

App smash Seesaw with iPad camera and ChatterPix Kids: Students record short video reflections or role-play scenarios where they demonstrate respectful friendship strategies or personal safety choices, then upload to Seesaw for peer feedback and self-assessment.

Students work in teams to design and build a physical obstacle course using recycled materials, cardboard, string, and simple craft supplies. Each obstacle represents a real-life challenge (e.g., friendship conflict, online safety, learning a new skill). Teams embed Sphero Indi cars or Bee-Bots as 'resilience robots' that must navigate the course, with students programming the robots to model positive strategies (e.g., pausing to reflect, asking for help). Along the way, students craft interactive checkpoints?such as spinning wheels with growth mindset affirmations, or bridges that open when a team shares a respectful friendship tip. The project follows the Design Cycle: empathize (identify common challenges), define (choose obstacles), ideate (brainstorm course and checkpoints), prototype (build and test obstacles), and reflect (journal and peer feedback on strategies for overcoming setbacks).

plannerText: This unit explores how individuals respond to challenges and setbacks, develop personal strategies to manage change, and take responsibility for their own safety both online and face-to-face. Students investigate growth mindset, resilience, and respectful friendships through reflective journals, scenario-based activities, and collaborative projects, with strong links to social-emotional learning and student agency.

ci: Technology transforms how we create and share ideas.
lo: How technology expands creative possibilities; The evolution of communication tools; The impact of technology on expression

s.d: Students use 'Minecraft: Shipwreck Narrative' to collaboratively design a mysterious shipwreck scene, then write and record a digital narrative from multiple perspectives (e.g., survivor, rescuer, historian). They can embed clues, hidden messages, or QR codes in the Minecraft world, transforming storytelling into an immersive, interactive digital experience that blurs the line between author and audience.

Students create interactive digital books that showcase how communication tools have evolved, embedding audio interviews with family members, video reflections, and original illustrations. They can publish and share their books with younger students, demonstrating how technology transforms both the creation and sharing of ideas.

Learners design a series of digital posters or infographics that compare historical and modern communication tools, highlighting ethical considerations and cybersafety tips. They use Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to remix their own photos into creative digital collages.

Students document their learning journey by recording video reflections, uploading digital artefacts, and responding to teacher prompts about the impact of technology on creativity and communication. They can also peer-assess each other's digital projects, fostering a collaborative, reflective digital community.

Students design and distribute a digital survey to teachers and peers about technology use and creative expression. They analyse the data in Excel, then present their findings as part of a discussion on how technology shapes communication in their community.

In the Makerspace, students build physical prototypes of communication tools from different eras (e.g., telegraph, early radio, smartphone) using cardboard, wires, bottle caps, and recycled materials. They create a hands-on 'museum' where visitors can interact with each device, compare their functions, and reflect on how each innovation transformed the way ideas are shared.

plannerText: In this unit, students explore the central idea 'Technology transforms how we create and share ideas.' Key activities include investigating the evolution of communication tools, analysing ethical and cybersafety considerations, surveying technology use in the school, and planning digital workshops for younger students. The unit culminates in students leading hands-on technology workshops for Prep?Year 3, applying their learning to real-world service and creative expression.
humanVerifiedResetReason: Suggestion edited, swapped, regenerated or changed after human verification

ci: Systems connect us.
lo: Human systems; The interconnections that exist within and across systems; The influence of systems on communities

s.d: Students use Delightex to collaboratively design and simulate a real-world human-made system (e.g., a school canteen, public transport, or recycling system). They map out the parts, connections, and possible disruptions, then test 'what if' scenarios to see how changes ripple through the system, directly experiencing the central idea that 'systems connect us.'

Students design and distribute a digital survey using Microsoft Forms to investigate which systems (transport, waste, communication, etc.) are most important to their local community. They analyse the results in Excel, identifying patterns and presenting their findings on how systems influence daily life.

Students create a collaborative Padlet where they post photos, diagrams, and short explanations of systems they observe at school, home, or in the community. They use arrows and comments to highlight interconnections between systems, building a visual map of how systems overlap and influence each other.

Students author a digital storybook in Book Creator that follows the journey of an object or person through multiple interconnected systems (e.g., a lunchbox from home to school and back, or a letter through the postal system). They use text, images, and audio to show how each system connects and what happens if one part fails.

Students explore 'The Hall of Inventions' in Minecraft Education, identifying key innovations that have shaped human systems (transport, communication, health, etc.). They then design and build their own invention in the game, explaining how it could connect or improve existing systems in their community.

In groups, students physically prototype a working model of a human-made system (e.g., a recycling sorting station, a mini public transport network, or a classroom job allocation system) using cardboard, popsticks, bottle caps, string, and other recycled materials. They must demonstrate how the parts interact, what happens when a part is removed or changed, and present their model to the class, highlighting the system's connections and vulnerabilities.

plannerText: Students begin by brainstorming and mapping systems in their lives, then investigate the form, function, and connections of both natural and human-made systems through hands-on observation, digital documentation, and collaborative inquiry. They explore how systems are interconnected, what happens when systems change or fail, and how human actions can influence or improve systems, culminating in sustainability projects and real-world action.

ci: Changes to the Earth impact environment and communities.
lo: The causes of geological change over time; How extreme environments cause natural disasters; The impact of natural events on communities

s.d: Transform your students into 'Natural Disaster News Reporters'! In this innovative project, Year 4s will research a real-world natural disaster (such as bushfires, floods, earthquakes, or volcanic eruptions) and create a dynamic multimedia news report using Adobe Express. Students will combine images, video clips (recorded with the iPad Camera app), voiceovers, and text to explain the causes of the disaster, its impact on environments and communities, and how people responded and adapted. This engaging digital storytelling approach empowers students to synthesize their learning, practice digital literacy, and creatively communicate complex scientific ideas to an authentic audience.

Have students document their hands-on STEM experiments (like the Earthquake Tower or Flood Simulation) using the iPad Camera app. They then upload annotated photos and video reflections to Seesaw, combining visual evidence with voice recordings to explain the science behind the disasters and how communities respond.

Students use Micro:bit to create animated models of volcanic eruptions, exploring geological changes and their impact on communities. They code sequences that simulate natural disasters, deepening their understanding of cause and effect in Earth's dynamic systems. (https://microbit.org/teach/lessons/?selected=volcano-animations-unit-of-work)

Students will become disaster engineers by designing and building earthquake simulators and flood-resistant structures using Lego Spike Prime. They'll test how different building techniques withstand simulated natural disasters, collect data on their designs' performance, and iterate to improve resilience. To showcase their findings, students will create dynamic presentations in Canva, combining photos, diagrams, and reflections to explain how communities can adapt to Earth's changing environments.

Connect the study of floods and community response to real-world engineering by having students use Minecraft: Filtering Water On The Iss (https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/lessons/filtering-water-on-the-iss). Students experiment with designing water filtration systems, linking to how communities recover from floods and ensure safe water supplies after disasters.

Students use the Design Cycle to collaboratively design, build, and test model communities that can withstand simulated natural disasters. First, they research real-world disasters (earthquakes, floods, bushfires) and brainstorm resilient features. Using physical materials like cardboard, popsticks, recycled plastics, clay, and fabric, they construct miniature buildings, bridges, and landscape features (e.g., levees, retaining walls). Integrating Sphero Indi robots or Bee-Bots, students code the robots to simulate the movement of floodwaters or seismic activity through their models. They observe how their structures respond, document the impact, and iterate their designs for improved resilience. Throughout, students capture their process with iPads (photos/videos), annotate their design choices, and present their final 'resilient community' prototypes to peers, reflecting on how technology and engineering can help communities adapt to Earth's changing environments.

plannerText: This unit engages Year 4 students in exploring how changes to the Earth, such as natural disasters, impact environments and communities. Through hands-on experiments, research projects, digital presentations, and case studies, students investigate the causes of geological change, the effects of extreme environments, and how communities respond and adapt to these challenges.

ci: Country, language and community are significant to the lives of First Nations people.
lo: The diversity of First Nations people; The importance of community, family and kinship; The importance of Country to First Nations people

s.d: Immerse your Year 4 students in an interactive exploration of cultural heritage with 'Peter Is Here Ai For Cultural Heritage' in Minecraft Education. Students will investigate the significance of Country, language, and community to First Nations people by virtually exploring, protecting, and celebrating cultural heritage sites. They'll collaborate to research Indigenous perspectives, reflect on the importance of place and language, and creatively build digital representations of Dreaming stories or significant landmarks. This hands-on experience empowers students to become digital custodians of culture, culminating in a class presentation using Canva or Adobe Express to share their discoveries and creative builds with the wider community.

Students research and document diverse First Nations groups around the world, then collaboratively build a multimedia Padlet wall with images, audio, and video reflections. They can app smash by recording their personal reflections or interviews on Seesaw and embedding them into the Padlet, creating a living digital tapestry of perspectives and traditions.

Design a visual dictionary of Woiwurrung language words, pairing each word with student-created illustrations and context sentences. These can be printed for display or shared digitally, supporting the school's goal of exposing the community to Aboriginal language and fostering respect for linguistic diversity.

Produce interactive digital storybooks retelling Dreaming stories or exploring the significance of Country, language, and community. Students can include audio narration, original artwork, and embedded video to make the stories accessible and engaging for a wider audience.

Students produce a series of audio stories and interviews exploring the significance of Country, language, and community to First Nations people, using Adobe Express Podcasting (https://podcast.adobe.com). They script, record, and edit their own podcasts to share Dreaming stories, language research, and reflections on Indigenous perspectives, culminating in a digital showcase for the school community. This authentic audio storytelling empowers students to amplify First Nations voices and deepen their understanding through creative communication.

Students work in teams to research and select key First Nations words, symbols, and stories connected to Country and community. Using recycled materials, clay, cardboard, and natural objects, they design and build a series of totem poles or 'story markers' that represent these ideas. Each totem incorporates a simple, student-built electronic sound module (Makey Makey or micro:bit with speakers) hidden inside, which plays a pre-recorded greeting, word, or short story in a local Indigenous language when touched. Students use iPads to record their audio and program the sound triggers. The completed Talking Totem Trail is set up as a physical path around the school grounds, inviting the community to interact with each totem, hear the language, and learn about its significance, blending digital storytelling with hands-on cultural making.

plannerText: This unit explores the significance of Country, language, and community to First Nations people, with students engaging in research, reflection, and creative activities such as mapping, language exploration, Dreaming stories, and artistic expression. Learning experiences include collaborative digital projects, visual and audio storytelling, and hands-on inquiry into Indigenous perspectives, culminating in authentic action and community sharing.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Human migration is a response to challenges and opportunities
lo: The reasons people migrate; Migration throughout history; Effects of migration on communities, cultures and individuals
plannerText: Not yet documented in PDF.

ci: Leaders shape communities.
lo: Roles and responsibilities of effective leadership; The purpose of leadership in communities; Qualities of influential leaders

s.d: Immerse your Year 4 students in the world of real-life changemakers! In 'Active Citizen', students will explore the inspiring stories of Nobel Peace Laureates and discover how leaders shape communities for the better. They'll journey through interactive Minecraft environments, meet virtual representations of influential leaders, and reflect on the qualities and actions that define effective leadership. After their digital exploration, students will collaborate to design their own 'Hall of Leadership' in Minecraft, showcasing the strengths and values they believe are essential for positive community impact. For their final presentation, students will use Canva to create vibrant digital posters highlighting their chosen leader and the leadership qualities they've learned about, connecting their Minecraft experience directly to the unit's central idea.

Students design and publish digital 'Leadership Qualities' infographics or posters, highlighting the strengths, responsibilities, and impacts of leaders they research in the unit. These can be shared with the school community to inspire others and reinforce the unit's focus on leadership roles and community impact.

Facilitate a digital leadership reflection journal on Seesaw, where students regularly post voice recordings, videos, or annotated images about their own leadership actions and observations in the school. This ongoing portfolio supports self-management and connects directly to the unit's emphasis on personal growth and responsibility as a leader.

Guide students to collaboratively author a digital book titled 'Leaders in Our Community', combining research, interviews, and creative writing. Each student contributes a chapter profiling a leader (local, national, or global), explicitly linking their findings to the roles, responsibilities, and qualities explored in the unit.

Students launch a 'Leadership Voices' podcast, interviewing community leaders and reflecting on the qualities that shape effective leadership. Using Adobe Express Podcasting (https://podcast.adobe.com), they script, record, and edit episodes that explore how leadership impacts communities, fostering authentic connections and communication skills.

Students form small 'leadership teams' to design and physically build a miniature community using recycled materials, cardboard, and craft supplies?featuring key spaces like a town hall, park, school, and homes. Each team programs a Sphero Indi robot to act as a 'leader' guiding citizens (represented by small figurines) through the community to solve challenges (e.g., organizing a clean-up, resolving a conflict, planning an event). Teams use the Design Cycle to empathize with community needs, ideate solutions, prototype their community layout and leadership pathways, test their robot's effectiveness as a leader, and refine both the physical build and coding. The project culminates in a showcase where teams explain how their robot's programmed 'leadership qualities' (e.g., clear communication, decision-making, empathy) helped shape and support their community.

plannerText: This unit explores the central idea 'Leaders shape communities' through inquiries into the roles and responsibilities of effective leadership, the purpose of leadership, and the strengths and qualities of a leader. Students engage in research, reflection, and creative activities to understand how leadership impacts communities, culminating in personal leadership applications and collaborative projects.

ci: People can create or manipulate messages to target specific audiences.
lo: The devices used to target audiences; Critical evaluation of messages; How we know we have been influenced

s.d: Transform your Year 5 students into media creators by having them design persuasive advertisements or campaign posters in Canva. Students critically analyse real-world media, then use Canva's powerful design tools?including the 'Remove Background' feature for their own photos (taken with the iPad Camera app)?to craft visually compelling messages targeting specific audiences. They'll experiment with colour, layout, and persuasive language, reflecting on how their choices influence beliefs and behaviours. This hands-on, creative process deepens their understanding of media manipulation and audience targeting, while building essential digital literacy and design skills.

Students become media creators by scripting, recording, and editing persuasive audio ads and interviews targeting specific audiences using Adobe Express Podcasting (https://podcast.adobe.com). They analyse real-world podcasts and advertisements, then craft their own episodes to explore how messages are shaped and manipulated. This hands-on experience brings critical media literacy to life and gives students a powerful platform to share their voices.

Students create a podcast series where they interview community members or experts about how media messages have influenced their beliefs or actions. Using GarageBand, they edit and layer in sound effects or music to intentionally shape the listener's emotional response, then reflect on the ethics of audio manipulation.

Students curate a collaborative Padlet gallery of advertisements, song lyrics, and political speeches, then use Seesaw to record video reflections or critiques. They annotate examples with digital ink, highlighting persuasive devices and discussing how each message could be interpreted differently by various audiences.

Students become digital reporters in 'Minecraft: The Investigators' (https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/lessons/the-investigators), where they must evaluate sources, decode clues, and uncover the truth behind three unique stories. This hands-on world challenges students to critically analyse bias, media manipulation, and the reliability of information in a gamified context.

Students work in teams to design and build physical 'message machines'?interactive arcade-style installations that deliver persuasive messages to a target audience. Using micro:bit (or Makey Makey) for simple inputs/outputs, students craft physical structures from cardboard, recycled materials, and craft supplies. Each machine features buttons, levers, or sensors that trigger lights, sounds, or moving parts programmed to convey a persuasive message (e.g., environmental awareness, healthy eating). Students prototype, test, and refine both the physical build and digital code, reflecting on how design choices (visuals, interactivity, language, effects) influence audience response. The final arcade is set up for peers to experience and evaluate the impact of each machine's message.

plannerText: This unit explores how people create or manipulate messages to target specific audiences, focusing on critical evaluation of media, persuasive devices, and the impact of communication choices. Students analyse mentor texts, advertisements, and campaigns, create their own persuasive media, and reflect on how messages influence beliefs and behaviours.

This hands-on world challenges students to critically analyse bias, media manipulation, and the reliability of information in a gamified context.","url":"https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/lessons/the-investigators"},{"t":"Persuasion Arcade: Interactive Message Machines","d":"Students work in teams to design and build physical 'message machines'?interactive arcade-style installations that deliver persuasive messages to a target audience. Using micro:bit (or Makey Makey) for simple inputs/outputs, students craft physical structures from cardboard, recycled materials, and craft supplies. Each machine features buttons, levers, or sensors that trigger lights, sounds, or moving parts programmed to convey a persuasive message (e.g., environmental awareness, healthy eating). Students prototype, test, and refine both the physical build and digital code, reflecting on how design choices (visuals, interactivity, language, effects) influence audience response. The final arcade is set up for peers to experience and evaluate the impact of each machine's message.","url":""}]

ci: Entrepreneurs have a responsibility to develop, market and sell sustainable products and services.
lo: The power of giving back; How our environment is impacted by the products we create; Sustainable business practices

s.d: Students will use Adobe Express to create dynamic, interactive marketing materials for their sustainable products or services. They can design eye-catching digital posters, infographics, and short promo videos that highlight their product's unique features, sustainability benefits, and environmental impact. These digital assets can be displayed at the market stall event via QR codes or on iPads, allowing students to practice real-world digital marketing skills and reflect on how entrepreneurs use media to influence consumer choices for a more sustainable future.

Create a digital marketing campaign for their sustainable product, including social media posts, infographics, and animated advertisements. Students use Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to create professional product images and app smash with iPad Camera for original photography.

Develop and distribute a market research survey to the school community to gather data on consumer preferences and sustainability awareness. Analyse the results in Excel to inform product design and marketing strategies.

Collaboratively build a digital 'Entrepreneurial Journey Wall' where students document each stage of their business development process, share reflections, and receive peer feedback. Include embedded videos, sketches, and pitch rehearsals.

Students explore the museum of key innovations, then design and build their own sustainable invention in Minecraft Education, justifying how it addresses real-world environmental or social needs. URL: https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/challenges/the-hall-of-inventions

Students work in teams to design and construct a physical miniature market stall from recycled materials (cardboard, fabric scraps, bottle caps, etc.), representing their sustainable product or service. They integrate a simple programmable microcontroller (e.g., Micro:bit or Makey Makey) to add interactive digital features?such as touch-activated customer feedback buttons, LED-lit product displays, or motion-triggered sound effects. Each stall must physically showcase their product prototype, include hand-crafted signage, and use the embedded tech to collect data or enhance the customer experience. The project follows the Design Cycle: empathize with customers, define needs, ideate stall features, prototype and test both physical and digital elements, and refine based on peer feedback before the final market event.

plannerText: In this unit, Year 5 students inquire into the responsibilities of entrepreneurs to develop, market, and sell sustainable products and services. They engage in market research, apply the design thinking process, prototype and test their ideas, and culminate in a market stall event where they pitch and sell their creations, reflecting on the impact of consumer choices and business practices on the environment.

ci: Design processes enable people to respond creatively to challenges in the natural world.
lo: The nature and impact of natural hazards (Causation); How design processes are used to generate and test solutions (Function); The ethical and practical considerations in human responses to natural challenges (Responsibility)

s.d: Students design interactive 3D models of natural hazards using Merge Cubes and bring them to life with Delightex, allowing peers to explore and annotate features such as volcanoes, earthquakes, or flood zones. This immersive app smash deepens understanding of how design processes respond to challenges in the natural world, as learners manipulate and present their models in augmented reality. The experience culminates in a virtual showcase where students explain their creative solutions and scientific thinking.

Learners engage with a teacher-created Wise Discussion Chatbot using the Socratic Tutor scenario, where the chatbot takes on the role of 'Dr Maya Chen, a disaster resilience engineer'. Students ask questions like 'How do engineers design buildings to withstand earthquakes?' or 'What mistakes have communities made when preparing for floods?' After the dialogue, each student produces a claim-evidence-reasoning response summarising how design thinking can reduce the impact of natural hazards.

Students collaborate in Minecraft: Moon Base One (https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/lessons/moon-base-one) to design and build a sustainable lunar base that responds to natural challenges like meteor impacts, extreme temperatures, and resource scarcity. Teams capture screenshots and brief explanations of their base?s hazard-resilient features for a class gallery walk.

Teams engineer and program Lego Spike Prime robots to simulate emergency response vehicles navigating through model disaster zones. Learners apply design processes to build, code, and test their robots? routes, reflecting on how creative engineering can solve challenges posed by natural hazards. This hands-on activity connects coding, spatial thinking, and ethical decision-making in disaster scenarios.

Throughout the inquiry, students document their design thinking journey in a multimedia Book Creator journal. They combine photos of prototypes, audio reflections, diagrams, and data visualisations to capture each stage of their process, culminating in a digital portfolio to share with peers and the wider community.

Teams investigate a specific natural hazard and use the Design Cycle to sketch, plan, and construct a disaster-resilient mini-home prototype. Students program a Sphero BOLT to simulate a 'family' navigating hazard scenarios (e.g., shaking for earthquakes, moving to higher ground for floods), testing and refining their designs against teacher-created events. The project culminates in a showcase where teams pitch their homes, demonstrating both engineering and coding solutions.

plannerText: This unit immerses Year 5 students in the design thinking process as they investigate natural hazards and human responses. Through provocations, data analysis, mapping, and hands-on prototyping, students develop and test creative solutions to real-world challenges, culminating in persuasive pitches and a community showcase.

ci: Finding peaceful solutions to conflict can make a difference in the world.
lo: Types of conflict; Causes and impacts of conflict; Conflict resolution

s.d: Learners create 3D conflict scenario dioramas on Merge Cube, then use Delightex to animate peaceful resolutions and dialogue between characters. This app smash empowers students to visualise and role-play conflict resolution strategies, making abstract concepts tangible as they explore causes, impacts, and solutions to conflict in local and global contexts. The interactive models spark rich discussion and reflection on how peaceful actions can change the world.

Students design and distribute a class survey using Microsoft Forms to collect peers' opinions on different types of conflict and effective peaceful resolutions. They analyse the collected data to identify common causes and impacts of conflict, then collaboratively create a summary report that highlights key findings and suggests strategies for conflict resolution aligned with the unit?s central idea.

Students create a 'Peace Talks' podcast, analysing protest songs, historical events, and personal stories of conflict resolution. Using Adobe Express Podcasting (https://podcast.adobe.com), they collaborate to script and record episodes that explore peaceful solutions to conflict, amplifying diverse perspectives and fostering empathy.

Students will work in teams to create an interactive digital 'Peace Pathways' infographic in Canva. Each group will research a real-world conflict (local or global), identify its causes and impacts, and then design a visually engaging flowchart that maps out peaceful conflict resolution strategies. Students will incorporate protest song lyrics, quotes from historical peacemakers, and their own reflections. The final Canva project will include clickable elements linking to student-created audio reflections or short video explanations, making the resource both informative and interactive. This empowers students to synthesise their learning and share actionable pathways to peace with the wider school community.

Students collaboratively explore the 'Peace Builders' Minecraft Education world (https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/lessons/peace-builders), engaging with stories of Nobel Peace Laureates and designing their own virtual spaces that promote dialogue, negotiation, and peaceful solutions to conflict. They reflect on how these digital environments can inspire real-world action.

s.t: Peace Pathways: Interactive Conflict Resolution Board
s.d: Students collaboratively design and build a large, physical board game that simulates real-world conflict scenarios and peaceful resolution strategies. Using cardboard, recycled materials, clay, and art supplies, they craft the game board, event cards, and player pieces representing diverse cultures and perspectives. Integrating Sphero Indi robots or similar, students program these robots to act as 'peacemakers' that move along the board based on players' decisions and outcomes. As part of the Design Cycle, students empathise by researching global and local conflicts, define key issues, ideate peaceful solutions, prototype their board and tech integrations, test play with classmates, and refine both physical and digital elements. This hands-on, hybrid project fosters creativity, critical thinking, and empathy by blending tangible making with interactive technology.

plannerText: This unit engages Year 5 students in exploring types, causes, and impacts of conflict, with a strong focus on peaceful conflict resolution both locally and globally. Learning experiences include analysing protest songs, picture books, and historical events, as well as creating collaborative digital resources and participating in practical conflict resolution activities.
