ci: The design and use of products can have an impact on the natural world.
lo: How material properties determine their use; Material solutions for a sustainable future; How research solves problems in the natural world

s.d: Students design interactive 3D models of their animal enrichment prototypes using Merge Cubes, then document the design process, material choices, and environmental impact in a digital science journal on Book Creator. This app smash allows students to virtually 'hold' and explore their creations while reflecting on sustainable design choices.

Students explore a sustainable building in Minecraft Education, analyzing how material choices and design decisions impact the environment. They can then reflect on how these principles could be applied to their own enrichment toy prototypes.

Students investigate sustainable product design by building and testing prototypes with Micro:bit, using the 'Electrical Conductors' lesson (https://microbit.org/teach/lessons/?selected=electrical-conductors-unit-of-work). They explore how material properties affect conductivity and product function, linking science, design, and environmental impact. This hands-on challenge makes STEM learning relevant and exciting.

Students program Sphero BOLT robots to simulate animal movement and interaction with different enrichment toy prototypes. This hands-on coding activity helps them test and iterate their designs for functionality and animal engagement.

Students design and build animal enrichment devices using Lego Spike Prime, exploring how different material properties affect their creations? functionality and sustainability. They program their models to simulate animal interactions, testing how their designs respond to movement and behaviour patterns. This hands-on activity deepens understanding of product design impact on the natural world while fostering creativity and computational thinking.

Students use the Design Cycle to invent and build interactive animal enrichment games inspired by arcade machines, focusing on sustainability and animal wellbeing. Working in teams, students brainstorm animal needs, sketch ideas, and prototype enrichment devices using recycled cardboard, plastic bottles, fabric, string, and natural materials. They integrate programmable Bee-Bots or Sphero Indi robots as 'moving parts'?for example, a Bee-Bot could trigger a treat dispenser or reveal a hidden toy when an animal interacts with the device. Teams test and refine their creations, documenting iterations in their digital science journals, and reflect on how their material choices impact both function and environmental footprint.

plannerText: Students investigate how material properties influence product design, research sustainable solutions, and document their learning in digital science journals. They experiment with different materials, compare animal enrichment toys, and ultimately design and prototype their own enrichment device for animals, considering both functionality and environmental impact.
humanVerifiedSuggestionSignature: [{"t":"Merge Cubes","d":"Students design interactive 3D models of their animal enrichment prototypes using Merge Cubes, then document the design process, material choices, and environmental impact in a digital science journal on Book Creator. ci: Country, language and community are significant to the lives of First Nations people.
lo: Diversity of First Nations people; Importance of community, family and traditions; Importance of Country to First Nations people

s.d: Students create interactive Dreamtime stories or virtual dioramas using Merge Cubes, overlaying 3D symbols, animals, and landscapes significant to First Nations cultures. They then app smash by documenting their creative process and cultural reflections in Book Creator, combining AR visuals, text, and audio explanations to deepen understanding of Country, language, and community.

Design a collaborative digital mural in Canva where each student contributes a panel representing an aspect of First Nations identity, such as language, Country, or community. Use Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to incorporate student photos or artwork, and layer in authentic Indigenous symbols and patterns for a visually rich, respectful mural.

Build a living digital map on Padlet where students pin locations of different First Nations groups, add audio snippets of local languages, and share research on traditions, family structures, and connections to Country. Encourage students to comment and ask questions to foster inquiry and respectful dialogue.

Students record video reflections or interviews with family members about their own connections to place, language, and community, then compare and contrast with First Nations perspectives. Use Seesaw to curate a class gallery of these stories, highlighting diversity and shared values.

Students explore how First Nations peoples' knowledge of Country supports biodiversity by building 'beetopias' in Minecraft Education. They apply Indigenous perspectives on land stewardship and sustainable habitats, then reflect on the importance of community and tradition in environmental care.

Students work in teams to design and build a large-scale, winding river diorama using recycled materials, clay, cardboard, natural objects, and paint, representing the importance of waterways in First Nations cultures. Along the river, students craft significant landmarks, flora, and fauna, inspired by Dreamtime stories and Indigenous art. Integrating Sphero Indi robots, students code cultural storytelling 'journeys'?the robots follow hand-crafted, painted paths along the river, stopping at key points where QR codes or NFC tags (embedded in crafted rocks or trees) trigger audio narrations, student-created digital artworks, or Dreamtime story segments on iPads. The project blends physical making, coding, and digital storytelling, deepening understanding of Indigenous connections to Country and water.

plannerText: Students explore the diversity, traditions, and connections of First Nations people to Country, language, and community through Dreamtime stories, art, excursions, and hands-on experiences. They create narratives, dioramas, and digital projects to synthesise their learning about Indigenous perspectives and cultural significance.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Exploration leads to discoveries, opportunities and new understandings.
lo: Reasons for exploration; How people have explored over time; Consequences of exploration

s.d: Students embark on a virtual 'Explorer's Museum' scavenger hunt using ClassVR headsets and Merge Cubes. They interact with 3D artefacts from different eras of exploration, unlocking clues and stories about famous explorers, navigation tools, and discoveries. Students then create a digital timeline of exploration by capturing screenshots and narrating their findings in Seesaw.

Students explore the Hall of Inventions world, investigating key innovations that enabled exploration (e.g., compass, sextant, ships). They select a blank plot to design and build their own invention that could have changed the course of history, then present their creation and its imagined impact to the class.

Students research a historical explorer or expedition and create an interactive digital book. They include maps, timelines, diary entries, and multimedia elements (audio, video, images) to bring the journey to life, then publish and share their books with peers for feedback.

Students design visually engaging infographics or posters that compare exploration methods and technologies across different eras. They use Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to integrate themselves into historical scenes, then annotate their posters with key discoveries and consequences.

Students plot the routes of famous explorers using Google Maps, adding custom pins with facts, images, and questions at significant locations. They analyse how geography, environment, and technology influenced the paths taken and present their interactive maps to the class.

s.t: Physical Makerspace Project: Cardboard Navigation Tools
s.d: Students work in teams to research and physically prototype historical navigation tools such as astrolabes, compasses, or sextants using cardboard, string, bottle caps, and recycled materials. They test their creations outdoors by simulating navigation challenges, reflecting on the limitations and breakthroughs of early explorers.

plannerText: This unit engages Year 4 students in investigating the reasons for exploration, how people have explored over time, and the consequences of exploration. Students analyse historical and modern exploration through mapping, research, narrative writing, and hands-on activities, culminating in creative presentations and physical prototypes.
humanVerifiedResetReason: Suggestion edited, swapped, regenerated or changed after human verification

ci: People's beliefs contribute to the diversity of human life.
lo: Spiritual or religious beliefs; Similarities and differences of spiritual beliefs; Contributions of different religions to human diversity

s.d: Students create interactive 3D Merge Cube experiences representing different places of worship or symbols from world religions. They then app smash by embedding videos, audio reflections, and AR screenshots into a digital Book Creator 'Belief Museum', allowing students to virtually 'hold' and explore diverse beliefs in their hands.

Students create a series of short podcasts where they interview family members or classmates about their spiritual or religious beliefs and traditions, then reflect on how these contribute to human diversity. Learners script questions, record audio, and edit their episodes to share respectful stories that deepen intercultural understanding and empathy.

Students design visually stunning infographics comparing the core beliefs, symbols, and practices of major world religions. Use Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to create digital collages of places of worship, and present findings in a class 'Belief Gallery Walk'.

Curate a playlist of short documentaries and virtual tours of places of worship from Clickview. Students use the platform's interactive video features to annotate, ask questions, and reflect on similarities and differences between spiritual practices.

Students record video interviews with family members or community leaders about their beliefs and traditions using the iPad camera. They upload and annotate these interviews in Seesaw, tagging key vocabulary and reflecting on how beliefs shape identity.

Students work in teams to research different world religions and spiritual beliefs, identifying key symbols, artefacts, and values. Using recycled materials (cardboard, fabric, string, clay, etc.), each group physically builds a symbolic 'island' representing a belief system?incorporating meaningful structures, colours, and objects. Next, teams collaboratively design and construct 'bridges' (using craft sticks, LEGO, or connectors) that link the islands, symbolizing shared values or respectful connections between beliefs. The bridges feature simple programmable elements: students embed Sphero Indi cars or similar robots, coding them with colour tiles to travel across the bridges and stop at key points where students record short explanations (using iPads to trigger audio via QR codes or NFC tags attached to the bridges). This hands-on project blends physical creativity, digital storytelling, and robotics, helping students visualize and celebrate the diversity and interconnectedness of human beliefs.

plannerText: This unit explores how people's beliefs contribute to the diversity of human life, focusing on spiritual and religious beliefs, similarities and differences among religions, and the impact of these beliefs on human diversity. Students engage in gallery walks, virtual tours, research, and creative projects to deepen understanding and foster respect for different worldviews.

ci: People design for purpose.
lo: How design thinking creates in various ways; Design thinking meets community needs; Effective ways to engage an audience

s.d: Students design and prototype interactive 3D models of disaster-resistant shelters or emergency tools using Tinkercad, then bring their creations to life with Merge Cubes and the Merge Explorer app. They can record AR video walkthroughs using the iPad camera, narrating their design thinking and how their solution meets a specific community need. This immersive, hands-on workflow makes abstract design concepts tangible and engaging.

Students create persuasive digital pitch decks for their UNICEF-inspired design solutions, using Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to showcase their prototypes and integrate infographics, maps, and empathy maps. This supports the pitch-writing and presentation phase, allowing students to visually communicate their process and impact.

Students become hazard heroes by designing and coding disaster response devices with Micro:bit, inspired by the 'Night Safety' lesson (https://microbit.org/teach/lessons/?selected=night-safety). They create wearable alerts or safety systems, connecting their design thinking to real-world community needs and resilience. This project brings STEM and social responsibility together in a dynamic, student-led way.

Teams publish interactive digital journals in Book Creator, combining photos, videos, voice recordings, and diagrams to document each stage of the design thinking process. This supports reflection, self-assessment, and sharing with the wider school community.

Students collaborate in teams to design and program Lego Spike Prime robots that simulate emergency response tasks related to natural hazards. They create functional prototypes that demonstrate purposeful design and problem-solving, applying the design thinking process to meet community needs. This hands-on activity brings the unit?s central idea to life by combining creativity, coding, and teamwork in authentic scenarios.

Students work in teams to identify a natural hazard affecting a community (e.g., flood, earthquake, bushfire) and design a hands-on rescue mission scenario. Using Sphero Indi robots as 'rescue vehicles,' students physically build a detailed hazard landscape with recycled materials?such as rivers, collapsed bridges, blocked roads, and shelters?on large cardboard bases. They craft movable barriers, construct safe zones, and create obstacles that mirror real-world challenges. Teams then program their Sphero Indi to autonomously navigate the custom-built terrain, deliver 'emergency supplies' (represented by small crafted tokens), and reach affected areas. Throughout, students document their design thinking process, test and iterate their physical and digital prototypes, and present their final rescue solutions to an audience, explaining how their design addresses both the hazard and community needs.

plannerText: In this unit, students explore how people design for purpose through the lens of natural hazards and community needs. They engage in the design thinking process, research real-world problems, collaborate in teams, prototype innovative solutions, and pitch their ideas to an authentic audience, integrating empathy, data analysis, and persuasive communication throughout.

ci: Economic systems are an integral part of human society.
lo: Wealth is more than money; Responsibilities when managing income; Income is earned and managed in many ways

s.d: Students design and build interactive 3D models of their 'ideal community' or economic system using Merge Cubes and the Merge Explorer app. They then use the iPad camera to record a virtual guided tour, narrating how wealth, resources, and responsibilities are distributed in their imagined society. This merges tactile, spatial, and digital storytelling for a futuristic approach to economic systems.

Students collaborate on a dynamic Freeform board to visually map and categorise diverse forms of wealth beyond money, using images, text, and drawings to represent their ideas about economic systems. They organise their thinking around the unit?s central idea by connecting personal, social, and cultural examples of wealth, creating a rich, interactive digital mural that evolves with their inquiry.

Students produce an 'Economic Voices' podcast, simulating interviews with historical and contemporary figures about economic systems, wealth, and responsibilities. Using Adobe Express Podcasting (https://podcast.adobe.com), they script, record, and edit episodes that bring economic concepts to life through storytelling and debate.

Students explore the evolution of economic systems by visiting the virtual museum in Minecraft: The Hall of Inventions. They research a key economic innovation (e.g., currency, banking, trade networks), then build their own invention in the museum, explaining its impact on society and connecting it to their classroom inquiry. This fosters historical perspective and creative application.

Students use Excel to model personal or community budgets, experimenting with different income sources, expenses, and savings strategies. They create interactive spreadsheets that visually demonstrate the consequences of financial decisions, supporting critical thinking about economic responsibility and resource allocation.

s.t: Wealth Web: Community Resource Exchange Game
s.d: Students use the Design Cycle to collaboratively invent and build a hands-on board game that models how communities organise, share, and grow different forms of wealth (not just money). In teams, they design a 3D 'community map' using cardboard, clay, and recycled materials to represent neighbourhoods, resources (like skills, time, food, knowledge), and opportunities. They then integrate programmable Sphero Indi robots as 'community couriers'?students code them to follow colour-coded tracks between locations, simulating the flow of resources and jobs. Game mechanics, designed and prototyped by students, include physical tokens, custom dice, and event cards, all crafted by hand. The final game is play-tested by peers, requiring players to cooperate, trade, and solve challenges to build a thriving, equitable community?making abstract economic concepts tangible and dynamic through creative making and digital-physical integration.

plannerText: Students inquire into economic systems by exploring wealth beyond money, responsibilities in managing income, and diverse ways income is earned and managed. They engage in provocations, debates, budgeting simulations, job applications, and collaborative projects to design ideal communities, culminating in creative presentations and hands-on design challenges that connect economic concepts to real-world contexts.

ci: The interactions between living things, materials, and light enable us to make decisions about the world
lo: How structural features and adaptations help living things survive; How weathering and erosion cause change to Earth's surface; How light is used in science and technology

s.d: Students create interactive 3D models of natural disasters (e.g., bushfires, earthquakes, floods) using Merge Cubes and the Merge Explorer app, then record AR video explanations using the iPad camera. They can app smash with Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to place themselves inside their AR scenes, narrating the science and human impact for a truly immersive digital artifact.

Students use Minecraft Education's 'Invisible Forces At Work' lesson to explore magnetism and gravity, then design a magnetic structure that could protect the International Space Station or assist astronauts. This directly connects to the unit's focus on the interactions between materials and forces in the real world.

Students engineer early warning wearables for natural disasters using Micro:bit, guided by the 'Night Safety' lesson (https://microbit.org/teach/lessons/?selected=night-safety). They code devices that simulate alerts for bushfires, floods, or earthquakes, linking science, technology, and community safety. This hands-on project empowers students to turn empathy into action through innovation.

Students collaboratively create a digital 'Wonder Wall' on Padlet by posting questions, research findings, and Clickview video clips about natural hazards such as bushfires, earthquakes, and floods. They use Padlet?s mapping feature to build a labelled visual map that tracks the locations and impacts of these disasters across Australia, connecting directly to the unit?s central idea about interactions between living things, materials, and light. This activity supports inquiry into how natural forces cause change to Earth's surface and helps students analyse data to make informed decisions about protecting communities.

Students collect and organise data on natural disasters such as bushfires, floods, and earthquakes using Microsoft Excel. They create dynamic charts and graphs to visualise patterns and trends, helping them analyse risks and make informed decisions about community safety. This hands-on activity connects the central idea by exploring how data-driven insights support disaster preparedness and resilience.

Students use the Design Cycle to invent and prototype wearable early warning devices that help communities respond to bushfires, earthquakes, or floods. First, they research real-world warning systems and brainstorm features that could alert and protect children during disasters. In teams, students physically craft wearable prototypes (e.g., wristbands, badges, or headbands) using materials like felt, recycled plastics, Velcro, LEDs, conductive thread, and cardboard. They integrate a simple programmable sensor (such as a Makey Makey or Circuit Playground Express) to simulate detecting a hazard?triggering lights, sounds, or vibrations. Students code their device (e.g., using block-based coding) to respond to 'danger signals' (like shaking for earthquakes or a heat source for bushfires). The project culminates in a showcase where students pitch their wearable's design, demo its warning features, and reflect on how it could uphold children's rights and safety in disaster-prone areas.

plannerText: This unit immerses Year 5 students in the science of natural disasters, focusing on bushfires, earthquakes, and floods. Through collaborative inquiry, students research hazards, analyse data, map risks, and use the design thinking process to prototype and pitch solutions that protect communities and uphold children's rights. The unit integrates maths, science, geography, and digital technologies, culminating in student-led presentations and reflections.

ci: Communities interact with the environment in different ways.
lo: The need for conservation; Community values inform management of the natural environment; Ways environmental stewardship can drive change

s.d: Students create interactive 3D models of local flora and fauna using the Merge Cube and iPads, then use the Merge Explorer app to overlay digital information about conservation needs and stewardship actions. They can record video tours of their models using the iPad camera, narrating how their community can interact with and protect these species.

Students collaboratively research and document local council sustainability initiatives on Padlet, then design persuasive digital posters in Canva to promote their chosen initiative to the school community, integrating real data and student-generated visuals.

After a nature walk around the school, students use Book Creator to compile a multimedia field guide of native plants and animals, including photos, audio reflections, and conservation tips tailored to their local environment.

Students explore how bees interact with their environment by applying their knowledge of stewardship and conservation to design and build digital 'beetopias' in Minecraft, supporting thriving bee colonies and reflecting on the importance of biodiversity.

Students program Micro:bit devices to collect and display real-time environmental data, such as temperature or moisture levels, simulating how communities monitor local ecosystems. They create interactive models that demonstrate the impact of conservation efforts on their local environment, linking directly to the unit?s focus on stewardship and community values. This hands-on coding activity encourages scientific inquiry and environmental responsibility.

Students work in teams to design and construct a large, modular river ecosystem model using recycled cardboard, clay, fabric, and natural materials. They research local waterways and First Nations perspectives to inform the ecosystem's features?such as wetlands, native plant zones, and animal habitats. Integrating Sphero Indi robots, students program the robots to represent 'water flow' or animal migration, navigating through their hand-built landscape. As part of the Design Cycle, students iteratively test and refine their physical models to ensure the 'water' can move through the system without blockages, simulating the impact of pollution, conservation efforts, and community interventions. The final ecosystem includes interactive elements (e.g., removable pollution sources, movable animals, or plant growth markers) and is presented to the school community with student-led explanations of conservation choices and stewardship strategies.

plannerText: Students inquire into how communities interact with the environment, focusing on conservation, stewardship, and the influence of community values on environmental management. Key activities include exploring First Nations perspectives, researching local flora and fauna, investigating council initiatives, and pitching persuasive sustainability actions for the school community.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: The discovery of resources has impacted the growth and development of nations.
lo: Significance of resource discovery in development of societies; Impact of migration on cultural, social and economic growth; Exploration and settlement effects on Indigenous and colonial populations

s.d: Students create interactive 3D artefacts representing key resources discovered in Australia (e.g., gold, wheat, wool) using Merge Cube. They then app smash by embedding their 3D models and explanatory videos into a digital Book Creator journal, allowing for immersive storytelling about how each resource shaped the nation's growth.

Launch a collaborative migration map: students research and post digital 'story cards' on a Padlet world map, each card representing a migrant group or individual who contributed to Australia's development, including their reasons for migration and the impact on society.

Learners create a Gold Rush podcast series, narrating stories from the perspectives of migrants, Indigenous Australians, and colonial settlers. Using Adobe Express Podcasting (https://podcast.adobe.com), students blend research, interviews, and dramatic storytelling to explore how resource discovery shaped nations and changed lives.

Students explore the Minecraft: The Hall of Inventions world, then select a historical Australian invention or resource. They research its significance and build a new exhibit in Minecraft, presenting how this resource or invention contributed to national growth.

Students program Micro:bit devices to simulate communication methods used during the Gold Rush, creating interactive messages that represent how prospectors and settlers shared information about resources and migration. They design simple code to send alerts or directions, connecting technological innovation to the social and economic growth of nations. This hands-on activity brings history and coding together, deepening understanding of the unit?s central idea.

s.t: Gold Rush Innovation Trail: Interactive Prospecting Adventure
s.d: Students work in teams to design and build a large, physical 'prospecting trail' inspired by Australia's goldfields, using recycled materials, cardboard, clay, and fabric to create realistic terrain, dig sites, rivers, and historical landmarks. Integrating Ozobot robots, students program them to represent gold-seekers navigating the trail, encountering challenges such as Indigenous land boundaries, environmental obstacles, and encounters with other migrants. Each stop features student-made, interactive artifacts (e.g., pop-up tents, sluice boxes, mine entrances) that trigger physical mechanisms (levers, pulleys) or QR codes for digital mini-narratives. The project follows the Design Cycle: students research gold rush history, ideate trail features and challenges, prototype structures and mechanisms, test Ozobot navigation, and refine both physical and digital elements for an immersive, hands-on exploration of how resource discovery shaped migration, innovation, and impact on communities.

plannerText: This unit explores how the discovery of resources has impacted the growth and development of nations, focusing on Australia's Gold Rush, migration, and the effects on Indigenous and colonial populations. Students investigate historical events, analyze cause and effect, research case studies, and create timelines, infographics, and perspective-based narratives to deepen their understanding of economic, social, and environmental change.

ci: Managing change requires acceptance, knowledge and strategies.
lo: Establishing, managing and maintaining relationships; Managing behaviours in digital situations; Managing changes associated with puberty

s.d: Students create digital self-portraits using iPads, then use Merge Cubes to transform their artwork into interactive 3D AR experiences. Each cube face can represent a different aspect of their identity or a strategy for managing change, allowing peers to explore and 'unlock' stories about acceptance, growth, and resilience in an immersive gallery walk.

Students create interactive digital wellbeing journals using Explain Everything, combining drawings, text, and voice recordings to reflect on managing change and emotional responses. They animate self-portraits or symbols to express personal insights about acceptance and strategies for navigating puberty and digital behaviours. This dynamic multimedia project deepens their understanding of the central idea by making their reflections engaging and shareable.

Students collaboratively design empathy map posters in Canva, visually representing the feelings, needs, and strategies of someone experiencing change (e.g., starting at a new school, going through puberty, or facing online challenges). These can be shared digitally or printed for classroom discussion.

Students create and publish digital sticky notes on a shared Padlet board, each detailing a strategy, resource, or personal story related to managing change in relationships, digital behaviours, or puberty. They actively comment on and 'like' peers? posts, collaboratively building a dynamic resource bank that reflects their growing knowledge and acceptance of change. This interactive wall serves as a visual and evolving product that connects directly to the unit?s central idea of managing change through acceptance, knowledge, and strategies.

Each student authors a multimedia eBook in Book Creator, combining text, images, audio reflections, and video clips to document their learning about managing change, acceptance, and growth. The final product serves as a personal guide and can be shared with younger students or families.

s.t: Transitions Time Capsule: Interactive Memory Vaults
s.d: Students use the Design Cycle to collaboratively design and build physical 'time capsules' from recycled materials, cardboard, and craft supplies, each representing a key transition or change in their lives (e.g., moving schools, making new friends, learning new skills). Each capsule features a secret compartment and a digital element: students embed programmable micro:bit devices (or similar) to add interactive features such as sound messages, LED patterns, or simple digital counters that activate when the capsule is opened. Students prototype, test, and iterate both the mechanical and digital components, reflecting on the emotions and strategies associated with change. The final interactive vaults are shared in a class 'Transitions Gallery,' where peers unlock, explore, and discuss the stories and wellbeing strategies hidden inside each capsule.

plannerText: This unit explores how managing change requires acceptance, knowledge, and strategies, with a focus on relationships, digital behaviours, and personal growth. Students engage in reflective journaling, digital art, empathy mapping, and collaborative discussions, culminating in creative projects that support wellbeing and positive transitions.

ci: The media engages its audience and invites a response
lo: How media targets audiences; Techniques used to engage and persuade; Critical evaluation of media messages
plannerText: Not yet documented in PDF.

After exploring the world and chatting with Alexis, students summarise their new insights in a reflection, deepening their understanding of how media shapes consumer choices and ethical decision-making.","url":""},{"t":"Padlet","d":"Students use Padlet to collaboratively brainstorm and map out the influences on their chosen product, then design and distribute a Microsoft Forms survey to gather real data on peer influences and advertising effectiveness. This workflow mirrors the unit's research and analysis tasks, making student inquiry visible and actionable as they interpret survey results and update their Padlet with evidence-based conclusions.","url":""},{"t":"Adobe Express Podcasting","d":"Learners script and record persuasive audio advertisements or slam poems critiquing media influence and ethical consumerism using Adobe Express Podcasting (https://podcast.adobe.com). Their podcasts are published as part of a class 'Media Gallery', sparking discussion about the power of audio storytelling to shape opinions and drive action.","url":""},{"t":"Canva","d":"Students create dynamic, data-driven infographics and digital posters that visually communicate the impact of advertising strategies, peer influence, and environmental consequences of consumer choices. Canva's 'Remove Background' feature can be used to remix product images, allowing students to reimagine familiar ads and highlight ethical alternatives.","url":""},{"t":"Minecraft Education","d":"Students use the verified lesson 'Media Literacy: News and Bias' (https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/lessons/media-literacy-news-and-bias) to investigate how media sources use persuasive techniques and bias to influence audiences. They present their findings by building in-game newsrooms and posting screenshots with critical reflections on advertising claims.","url":""},{"t":"Makey Makey","d":"Persuasion Arcade: Build & Code an Interactive Ethical Shopping Game?students work in teams to design and construct a physical 'arcade booth' using recycled materials, LED lights, and simple circuits. Using Makey Makey or Micro:bit, they program interactive buttons that trigger digital media (audio jingles, persuasive slogans, or ethical dilemma videos) on an iPad, following the Design Cycle to prototype, test, and refine their booth, then reflect on how their arcade influences thinking about ethical consumerism.","url":""}]

ci: An inquiry into the interconnectedness of human-made systems and communities; the structure and function of organisations.
lo: Lines of inquiry to be confirmed once planner is uploaded

s.d: Students create 3D models of interconnected human-made systems?like supply chains or transportation networks?on Merge Cube, then use Delightex to animate the flow of goods, information, and influence. This app smash brings abstract systems thinking to life, allowing learners to manipulate variables and observe ripple effects in real time. The activity supports inquiry into how advertising, peer influence, and ethical choices shape communities and consumer behaviour.

Groups create and distribute digital surveys to the school community to gather authentic data on peer influences, advertising effectiveness, and ethical considerations in consumer choices. Students then analyse the results in Excel to identify trends and present their findings as part of their inquiry into how organisations shape consumer behaviour.

Students design and code ethical pop-up market systems using Micro:bit, inspired by the 'Data Handling' lesson (https://microbit.org/teach/lessons/?selected=data-handling-unit-summary). They gather and analyse data on consumer choices, simulate transactions, and reflect on the impact of their decisions. This project fuses economics, ethics, and digital innovation.

Classes use Padlet to crowdsource and visually map the production, marketing, and disposal journeys of selected products. Each group posts images, research, and ethical/environmental findings, allowing for a collaborative, interactive exploration of the structure and function of organisations within global systems.

Students use Google Maps to trace and visualise the global journey of a product, identifying key locations such as production sites, shipping routes, and retail outlets. They create interactive maps that highlight ethical and sustainable choices within supply chains, deepening their understanding of interconnected human-made systems and the impact of consumer decisions. Students use Street View to observe ground-level evidence, compare places and capture notes that connect directly to the unit.

s.t: Ethical Pop-Up Market: Build & Code Smart Stalls
s.d: Students work in teams to design, build, and code a physical 'pop-up market' stall that showcases a product with ethical and sustainable features. Using recycled materials, cardboard, and craft supplies, each group constructs a stall with interactive elements (e.g., spinning wheels, product displays, moving signs). They embed simple programmable electronics (such as micro:bit or Makey Makey) to add digital features like sound effects, scrolling LED messages, or touch-activated info panels that communicate the ethical story behind their product. The design cycle guides students through empathising with ethical consumers, ideating fair trade or eco-friendly product concepts, prototyping their stall, testing interactive features, and refining their presentation. The final market is set up in the Makerspace, where peers and visitors can 'shop', interact with the stalls, and reflect on how advertising, peer influence, and ethical considerations shape consumer choices.

plannerText: This unit engages Year 6 students in an inquiry into the interconnectedness of human-made systems and communities, focusing on how advertising, peer influence, and ethical considerations shape consumer choices. Students collaboratively research, analyse, and present findings on selected products, create persuasive multimodal texts, and explore alternative models of consumerism that honour fairness and sustainability.

ci: Scientists undertake experimentation to confirm, contest and create new theories.
lo: The scientific method; How experiments lead to new understandings; The relationship between planets and the Sun; Energy transfer and transformation in circuits

s.d: Students use Micro:bit to collect and analyse data from scientific experiments, bringing the scientific method to life as they investigate planetary motion or energy transfer. They visualise their findings and draw conclusions, deepening their understanding of how experimentation leads to new theories. (https://microbit.org/teach/lessons/?selected=data-handling-unit-summary)

Students create augmented reality models of electrical circuits, reversible/irreversible changes, or planetary systems using Merge Cubes and the Merge Explorer app. They can record short video explanations overlaying their AR models, demonstrating their understanding of scientific concepts and inquiry methods.

Students program and control Sphero BOLT robots to simulate planetary orbits and energy transfer in circuits, applying the scientific method to test hypotheses about motion and forces. They collect data from their robot?s sensors to analyse variables such as speed and distance, creating visual representations of their findings. This hands-on exploration deepens understanding of how experiments confirm and contest scientific theories in physical science and earth science.

Students design and distribute digital surveys using Microsoft Forms to collect data on classmates' hypotheses, experiment results, or scientific misconceptions. They analyze the data in Excel, drawing evidence-based conclusions and reflecting on the importance of data in scientific inquiry.

Students engage with the 'Minecraft: The Scientific Method' lesson (https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/lessons/the-scientific-method), where they plan and conduct experiments in a virtual world, practicing variables, fair testing, and data collection, directly linking to the unit's focus on how scientists experiment to build knowledge.

s.t: Planetary Motion Marble Run: Code & Construct
s.d: Students work in teams to design and build a large-scale, multi-level marble run that models planetary movement and energy transfer. Using recycled materials (cardboard, tubes, tape, popsticks), they physically construct tracks that represent orbits, inclines for gravitational slingshots, and obstacles for celestial events. Teams integrate micro:bit sensors (or similar age-appropriate microcontrollers) to digitally track marbles (planets) as they travel, displaying data (speed, distance, 'orbital period') on a connected tablet. Students program the micro:bit to trigger lights or sounds at key events (e.g., 'eclipse' zones, energy transfers). The project culminates in a science fair demonstration where students explain how their hybrid creation models scientific concepts of motion, energy, and planetary systems, and reflect on how scientists use both physical models and digital tools to test and refine theories.

plannerText: This unit immerses students in scientific inquiry through hands-on experiments in physical science, chemistry, and earth/space science. Students follow the scientific method to investigate energy transfer, chemical changes, and planetary movement, culminating in a science fair where they present their own experiments and reflect on how scientists confirm, contest, and create new theories.

ci: The influences on our consumer choices reveal the interdependence between human and natural systems and call for responsible action toward just and sustainable futures.
lo: Influences on consumer choices (function); How consumer decisions impact living things and their habitats (causation); Alternative models of consumerism that honour a fair and sustainable future (perspective)

s.d: Students collaboratively design immersive 3D virtual worlds in Delightex to visualize the entire lifecycle of a consumer product, from resource extraction to disposal. They then embed Padlet boards within their worlds for peers to leave critical feedback, questions, and alternative sustainable ideas, creating a dynamic, interactive exhibition that brings the complexities of consumer choices and sustainability to life.

Students create and distribute digital surveys using Microsoft Forms to gather real data from their peers and families about consumer habits, influences, and attitudes toward sustainability. They analyze the results to identify trends and present their findings as evidence in their group investigations.

Students use Micro:bit to collect and visualise data about their own consumer choices, exploring the interdependence between humans and natural systems. They analyse patterns and propose actions for more sustainable living, making data-driven decisions for real-world impact. (https://microbit.org/teach/lessons/?selected=data-handling-unit-summary)

Students compose and record original slam poems, raps, or jingles in GarageBand, using literary devices and sound design to creatively persuade listeners to reflect on their consumer habits and consider alternative, sustainable models.

Students explore the concept of recycling and sustainability in the 'Radical Recycling' Minecraft Education lesson, gaining hands-on experience with recycling systems and then reflecting on how these models could be adapted or improved in their own communities.

s.t: Sustainable Product Pitch: Smart Packaging Prototypes
s.d: Students work in teams to design, build, and prototype an innovative, eco-friendly product package that addresses an environmental issue caused by consumer choices (e.g., single-use plastics, food waste, over-packaging). Using recycled materials (cardboard, bottles, fabric, etc.), they construct a physical prototype of their packaging solution, integrating a micro:bit or Makey Makey to add an interactive digital feature (such as a touch sensor that plays a persuasive audio message, or a display showing the environmental impact of their design). Students use the Design Cycle: researching existing packaging, brainstorming and sketching solutions, building prototypes, testing with peers, and refining based on feedback. Each team creates a short video pitch using iPads, showcasing their prototype's features and sustainability benefits to a mock 'eco-investor' panel.

plannerText: Year 6 students investigate how influences shape consumer choices and the resulting impact on the world's sustainable future. They analyze advertising, peer influence, and environmental consequences through research, surveys, and case studies, culminating in collaborative presentations and creative action projects that advocate for just and sustainable consumer models.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Human needs drive the development of nations.
lo: How human needs drive national development; Role of migration in nation building; Impacts of development on Indigenous and colonial populations

s.d: Students build 3D migration journeys or nation-building timelines on Merge Cube, then use Delightex to animate key events, push-pull factors, and personal stories. This app smash helps learners visualise the forces that drive the development of nations and the lived experiences of migration, deepening empathy and historical understanding. The interactive models serve as powerful tools for sharing research and sparking discussion.

Create a digital timeline in Canva that visually compares pre- and post-colonised Australia, layering key events, migration waves, and changes in citizenship rights. Students can app smash by incorporating iPad camera photos from their Melbourne Museum excursion and use Canva's 'Remove Background' feature for artefact images.

Facilitate a collaborative Padlet where students post and respond to migration stories, personal family histories, and reflections on citizenship. They can link to research, videos, and maps, fostering a living digital archive that connects classroom learning to real-world experiences.

Students author a multimedia eBook in Book Creator, weaving together first-person migration narratives, maps, and primary source analysis. They can include voice recordings, scanned artefacts, and interactive quizzes about the influence of systems on citizens.

Design and distribute a Microsoft Forms survey to the school community, gathering data on migration backgrounds, reasons for moving, and perceptions of Australian identity. Students analyse the results in Excel, then present their findings on how needs and systems influence migration and citizenship.

Students will research the journeys and experiences of diverse migrants to Australia, both pre- and post-colonisation. Using the Design Cycle, they will select a real or imagined migration story, then design and build a tangible, interactive 'memory machine'?a physical artifact that tells the story through movement, sound, and light. Students will craft the base using recycled materials, cardboard, and clay, constructing elements like turning wheels, pop-up scenes, or sliding panels. They will integrate simple programmable electronics (e.g., BBC micro:bit or Makey Makey) to trigger recorded oral histories, ambient sounds, or LED lights when parts are moved or touched. Each machine must physically embody key moments or challenges from the migration narrative, allowing viewers to 'travel' through the story by interacting with the device. This hands-on project encourages empathy, creativity, and a deep understanding of how migration shapes identity and nationhood.

plannerText: This unit explores how human needs drive the development of nations by comparing pre- and post-colonised Australia, examining the relationship between migration and needs, and analysing the influence of systems on citizens. Students engage in historical inquiry, personal narrative, museum excursions, and creative projects to understand migration, citizenship, and the shaping of Australian identity.

ci: An inquiry into the nature of the self; beliefs and values; personal, physical, mental, social and spiritual health; human relationships.
lo: Lines of inquiry to be confirmed once planner is uploaded

s.d: Learners create interactive 3D brain models or wellbeing maps on Merge Cube, then use Delightex to animate neural pathways, zones of regulation, or growth mindset strategies. This app smash makes neurodiversity and self-regulation visible, empowering students to explore how their brains work and how experiences shape learning and wellbeing. The activity supports reflection and collaborative discussion about personal growth and health.

Students create a collaborative digital wellbeing journal on Padlet, where they post daily reflections, strategies, and creative responses related to their personal health and mindset. This interactive space encourages peer feedback and shared learning about emotional regulation and neurodiversity, deepening their understanding of the central idea through ongoing dialogue and visual storytelling.

Students program their Micro:bit to build a step counter that tracks their daily physical activity by coding the accelerometer sensor to detect movement. They test and refine their code to ensure accurate step counts, then analyse their data to reflect on how physical activity supports their wellbeing, linking to the central idea of personal health and self-awareness.

Students create a digital 'Neurodiversity Storybook' in Book Creator, illustrating how different brains learn and thrive. Each page can feature a unique learning profile, myth-busting facts, and student-generated wellbeing tips, integrating research, creativity, and empathy.

Using Canva, students design vibrant infographics or posters that visually map the parts of the brain, their functions, and links to wellbeing strategies. They can use Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to personalize their posters with their own photos, making neuroscience relevant to their own identities.

s.t: Neurodiversity Quest: Build & Code Your Brain Adventure
s.d: Students work in teams to design and build a large, interactive 3D 'brain map' maze using cardboard, recycled materials, and craft supplies. Each section of the maze physically represents a different brain region or function (e.g., memory, emotion, creativity, self-regulation), decorated with symbols and textures. Students embed simple electronics (e.g., Makey Makey, LED circuits) to trigger sounds, lights, or digital messages when touched. Using Sphero Indi robots or similar, students code a 'neurodiverse explorer' to navigate the maze, encountering hands-on challenges and reflection prompts at each station (e.g., a puzzle about growth mindset, a sensory task for emotional regulation). The project culminates in a showcase where teams guide others through their custom brain adventure, explaining the links between physical structures, mental health, and personal identity.

plannerText: This unit explores identities as individuals and collectives through physical, emotional, social, and spiritual health and wellbeing, with a focus on the physiology and function of the brain, the impact of mindset and experiences on learning, and strategies to support health, safety, and wellbeing. Students engage in reflective, creative, and collaborative activities to deepen their understanding of neurodiversity, self-regulation, and personal growth.
